```mermaid
    L9 --> L10
    L10 --> L11[End User Semantic Insights]
```

### 4.7 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Ontology Language

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need inference and reasoning?} -->|No| Q2[Use RDFS]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Need rich class constraints?}
    Q3 -->|No| Q4[Use RDFS plus Simple Rules]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{Need maximum expressivity?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use OWL DL or OWL 2]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[Use OWL Lite or RDFS Plus]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define an Ontology. List and briefly explain its four essential components as per Gruber's formal definition.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 Mark):** An ontology is a *formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization* of a domain. It defines the vocabulary (terms, classes, properties) and the logical constraints that make the meaning machine-interpretable.
>
> **Four Essential Components (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Formal** — Encoded in a machine-readable logic language (e.g., OWL, Description Logic).
> 2. **Explicit** — Concepts, properties, and axioms are unambiguously stated.
> 3. **Shared** — Reflects a consensus of a community (e.g., FOAF, SNOMED CT).
> 4. **Conceptualization** — An abstract model capturing relevant entities and relations of a real-world domain.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q2. Differentiate between the Open-World Assumption (OWA) and Closed-World Assumption (CWA). Why is OWA essential for the Semantic Web?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | CWA | OWA |
> |---|---|---|
> | Missing fact | Considered **false** | Considered **unknown** |
> | Used in | Relational databases | Semantic Web / Ontologies |
> | Reasoning | Skeptical | Plausible / non-monotonic-tolerant |
>
> **Why OWA is essential (1.5 Marks):** The web is **incomplete and decentralized**. We cannot assume that just because a fact is not stated, it does not exist. For example, just because Arjun's `hasFriend Priya` is missing in one source does not mean they are not friends. OWA allows the system to remain consistent in the face of partial knowledge and supports **inference** of new facts from incomplete data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of the Semantic Web. With a neat diagram, describe the role of each layer and highlight how Ontologies (OWL) provide the semantic upgrade over RDF/RDFS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a small ontology for a *KTU Student Social Network*. Define at least **3 classes**, **3 object properties**, **1 datatype property**, and **2 subclass axioms**. Show how a reasoner would infer the class of a given individual.

---

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Semantic Web** is a vision of Tim Berners-Lee in which web data is given well-defined meaning, enabling machines and people to work in cooperation. It is structured as a **5-layer cake**:

1. **Layer 1 — URI / Unicode Foundation (1 Mark):** Provides the global unique identification of resources. URI ensures every entity has a globally unique address; Unicode ensures multilingual text support.
2. **Layer 2 — XML + XML Schema (1 Mark):** Provides *syntactic* interoperability — common syntax for structured documents. However, XML alone carries no semantics (tags can mean anything).
3. **Layer 3 — RDF + RDFS (1 Mark):** RDF introduces the **triple** model — `(s, p, o)` — providing the *first level of semantics*. RDFS adds vocabulary: `rdfs:Class`, `rdfs:subClassOf`, `rdfs:domain`, `rdfs:range`. This is **lightweight semantics**.
4. **Layer 4 — Ontology Vocabulary (OWL) (2 Marks):** OWL extends RDFS with **Description Logic** constructs — class equivalence, disjointness, cardinality, transitivity, inverse, functional properties. This enables **rich reasoning** and **automated inference**.
5. **Layer 5 — Logic, Proof, Trust (1 Mark):** Enables deductive reasoning, proof generation, and trust evaluation across heterogeneous sources.

**Semantic Upgrade — How Ontologies Transform RDF (1 Mark):**
RDF can say: *"Arjun hasFriend Priya."* — a flat statement.
An ontology (OWL) can additionally say:
- `hasFriend` is **symmetric** → if `Arjun hasFriend Priya`, then `Priya hasFriend Arjun`.
- `Student` is **disjoint with** `Professor` → an individual cannot be both.
- A `PostGradStudent` is a **subclass of** `Student` → reasoner automatically infers this.

Without OWL, these inferences are **impossible** to derive automatically. The ontology is therefore the **semantic engine** of the Semantic Web.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Step-by-Step Construction**

**Step 1 — Define Classes (1.5 Marks):**

```text
Person          (superclass)
Student         (subclass of Person)
PostGradStudent (subclass of Student)
Post            (independent class)
Group           (independent class)
```

**Step 2 — Define Object Properties (1.5 Marks):**

```text
hasFriend    : Person × Person        (Symmetric, Irreflexive)
authoredBy   : Post × Person          (Functional)
memberOf     : Person × Group         (Inverse: hasMember)
```

**Step 3 — Define Datatype Property (0.5 Mark):**

```text
hasGPA : Person → xsd:decimal
```

**Step 4 — Define Subclass Axioms (1 Mark):**

```text
PostGradStudent ⊑ Student ⊑ Person
PostGradStudent ⊑ ∃ hasGPA.xsd:decimal[≥ 7.5]
```

**Step 5 — Assert ABox Facts (1 Mark):**

```text
Arjun : PostGradStudent
Arjun hasGPA 8.2
Arjun hasFriend Priya
Priya : Student
```

**Step 6 — Reasoner Inference Output (1.5 Marks):**

| Inferred Fact | Reason |
|---|---|
| `Arjun : Student` | Subsumption: `PostGradStudent ⊑ Student` |
| `Arjun : Person` | Transitive subclass chain |
| `Priya hasFriend Arjun` | Symmetry of `hasFriend` |
| `Arjun memberOf X ⇒ X hasMember Arjun` | Inverse property |

The reasoner (e.g., **Pellet**, **Hermit**) classifies `Arjun` under `Person` and `Student` *automatically* — without explicit assertion.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of ontologies in the Semantic Web. Compare RDF, RDFS, and OWL in terms of expressivity and reasoning capability, with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain *Ontology-Based Knowledge Representation* with a real-world example from Social Network Analysis. Demonstrate how aggregation (e.g., computing the average degree centrality of a user's friend circle) is represented as a datatype property and constrained by ontological axioms.

---

> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Role of Ontologies (3 Marks):**
1. Provide a **common vocabulary** for a community (e.g., FOAF, Schema.org).
2. Enable **machine reasoning** via Description Logic.
3. Support **data integration** across heterogeneous social data sources.
4. Allow **semantic search** and **question answering** over the web.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | RDF | RDFS | OWL (DL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data model | Triple | Triple + class hierarchy | Triple + full logic |
| `subClassOf` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `domain` / `range` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Inverse properties | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Transitive properties | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Class disjointness | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Cardinality | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Reasoning | None | Basic | Full (DL Reasoner) |

**Example (1 Mark):** RDF states `<Arjun, hasFriend, Priya>`. RDFS adds `<hasFriend, rdfs:domain, Person>`. OWL adds `<hasFriend, owl:inverseOf, hasFriend>`, enabling automatic symmetric reasoning.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 Mark):** *Ontology-Based Knowledge Representation (OBKR)* is the practice of encoding the entities, relations, and rules of a domain in a formal ontology so that knowledge is *machine-understandable* and *machine-reasonable*.

**Real-world SNA Example (3 Marks):**
Consider a Twitter-like platform. We define:
- Classes: `User`, `Tweet`, `Hashtag`, `Trend`
- Properties: `posts(User, Tweet)`, `uses(Tweet, Hashtag)`, `follows(User, User)`

**Aggregation Step (2 Marks):**
For each user `u`, compute the **average follower count** of their followees:

$$ \text{avgFollowers}(u) = \frac{1}{\vert F(u) \vert} \sum_{v \in F(u)} \text{followerCount}(v) $$

We declare this as a datatype property:

$$ \text{avgFollowers} : \text{User} \rightarrow \text{xsd:integer} $$

**Ontological Constraint (1 Mark):**
We restrict `Influencer` users to have avgFollowers > 1000:

$$ \text{User} \sqcap \exists\,\text{avgFollowers}.\text{xsd:integer}[> 1000] \;\sqsubseteq\; \text{Influencer} $$

The reasoner then **automatically classifies** any user whose computed avgFollowers exceeds 1000 as an `Influencer` — closing the loop between **numerical aggregation** and **logical semantics**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Notes):**
>
> 1. **Confusing RDFS with OWL (–2 Marks):** RDFS does *not* support inverse, transitive, or functional properties. Always state OWL when these features are required.
> 2. **Mixing Object and Datatype Properties (–1.5 Marks):** Object properties have *classes* as range; datatype properties have *literals*. Examiners strictly test this.
> 3. **Forgetting the Open-World Assumption (–1 Mark):** In ontology reasoning, "not stated" ≠ "false." This is the #1 conceptual trap in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **No diagram in long answers (–1 to –2 Marks):** A neat labeled diagram of the Semantic Web layer cake or the ontology pipeline earns easy marks.
> 5. **Skipping the inference step (–1 Mark):** Always explicitly show *what the reasoner derives* from the given axioms and instance data. This is the heart of any ontology question.
> 6. **Mentioning "OWL = XML" (–1 Mark):** OWL is built *on top of* RDF/XML, but is a **logic-based** language, not a markup language. This confusion is a frequent red-flag for examiners.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📋 **High-Density Summary — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Ontology** = *formal, explicit, shared conceptualization* (Gruber's 4-word definition).
- **Three Pillars of Ontology:** Classes, Properties (Object + Datatype), Individuals.
- **RDF** = atomic unit is the **triple** `(s, p, o)`.
- **RDFS** = adds `subClassOf`, `domain`, `range` — *light* semantics.
- **OWL** = adds full **Description Logic** — rich semantics + reasoning.
- **Open-World Assumption** is the default in ontologies.
- **Closed-World Assumption** is the default in databases.
- **Semantic Web Layers (Bottom→Top):** URI → XML → RDF/RDFS → OWL → Logic/Proof/Trust.
- **Ontology Lifecycle:** Specification → Conceptualization → Formalization → Implementation → Maintenance.
- **Aggregation in OBKR** = compute a numeric/statistical feature (avg, sum, count) and represent it as a *datatype property* with logical constraints.
- **Reasoning Tasks:** Subsumption, Equivalence, Consistency, Instance Checking, Realization, Retrieval.
- **Common SNA Ontologies:** FOAF, SIOC, Schema.org, SKOS.
- **OWL Property Types:** Symmetric, Transitive, Functional, Inverse, Reflexive, Irreflexive.
- **Reasoner Tools:** Pellet, Hermit, FaCT++, ELK.
- **Query Language:** **SPARQL** (recursive acronym: SPARQL Protocol and RDF Query Language).
- **RDF Serializations:** RDF/XML, Turtle, N-Triples, JSON-LD.
- **Knowledge Graph** = Ontology (TBox) + Instance Data (ABox) + Reasoning.
- **For SNA:** Ontologies enable *semantic community detection*, *role inference*, and *influence aggregation* far beyond what raw graph analysis can do.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ontology Languages for the Semantic Web - Resource Description Framework, Web Ontology Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ontology Languages for the Semantic Web - Resource Description Framework, Web Ontology Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Ontology Languages for the Semantic Web

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Resource Description Framework (RDF)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The *Resource Description Framework (RDF)* is a W3C-standardized data model used for representing information about resources in the World Wide Web, particularly intended for representing metadata about web resources such as titles, authors, and modification dates. It is built on the mathematical concept of a **directed labeled graph** where every statement is a **Subject–Predicate–Object** triple.

**Key Properties of RDF:**
- **Subject** → the resource being described (identified by a URI)
- **Predicate** → the property or relationship (identified by a URI)
- **Object** → the value of that property (either a URI or a literal value)

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of RDF as a "social network for facts." Imagine a giant Excel sheet where every row is a single, atomic claim about the world. For example:
- "Kerala **isPartOf** India"
- "KTU **hasStudent** Arun"

Each row is a *triple*. RDF is the *universal language* of these claims, so two unrelated websites can exchange knowledge the way two strangers can exchange phone numbers — they both "speak RDF." In the context of Social Network Analysis, RDF is the **machine-readable backbone** that lets us describe a node (Person), an edge (friendOf), and node attributes (name, age) in a single, uniform model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** RDF is *schema-flexible* — meaning anyone can declare new properties without asking a central authority. This is the foundation that makes the **Semantic Web** "semantic."

---

### 1.2 Web Ontology Language (OWL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Web Ontology Language (OWL)* is a W3C-recommended language for defining and instantiating **Web ontologies**. An ontology is a formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization. OWL is layered on top of RDF/RDFS and provides additional vocabulary along with formal semantics based on **Description Logic (DL)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** If RDF is the Excel sheet of facts, then OWL is the *rule book* (a schema with teeth). For example, RDF can say "Arun knows Biju" and "Biju knows Arun" as two separate triples. OWL lets you state: *"The property `knows` is **Symmetric** — if A knows B, then B knows A."* OWL can also reason — given the fact that "All professors are employees" and "Dr. X is a professor," it can infer "Dr. X is an employee." In SNA, OWL is used to encode **rich social ontologies** (FOAF, SIOC) that allow automatic inference of hidden social connections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three OWL Sub-Languages** (must memorize for KTU):
> 1. **OWL Lite** → simple classification hierarchies, simple constraints.
> 2. **OWL DL** → maximum expressiveness while guaranteeing **computational completeness** and **decidability** (corresponds to Description Logic SHOIN(D)).
> 3. **OWL Full** → maximum expressiveness but **undecidable** (no guarantee of reasoning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of an RDF Statement

A **RDF triple** is the atomic unit of RDF. It is formally represented as:

$$
T = \langle s, p, o \rangle
$$

where:
- $s \in U \cup B$ (Subject — URI or blank node)
- $p \in U$ (Predicate — must be a URI, never a blank node or literal)
- $o \in U \cup B \cup L$ (Object — URI, blank node, or literal value)
- $U$ = set of **URIs**, $B$ = set of **Blank nodes** (anonymous resources), $L$ = set of **Literals** (data values)

A collection of triples forms a **RDF graph** $G = (V, E)$ where vertices $V = s \cup o$ and edges $E = p$ are labeled.

### 2.2 RDF/RDFS Vocabulary — Core Constructs

| Construct | Meaning in plain English |
|---|---|
| `rdf:type` | Declares that a resource is an instance of a class |
| `rdfs:Class` | Declares a resource to be a class |
| `rdfs:subClassOf` | States hierarchical class relationships |
| `rdfs:subPropertyOf` | States hierarchical property relationships |
| `rdfs:domain` | Specifies the class a subject must belong to |
| `rdfs:range` | Specifies the class a property's value must belong to |
| `rdfs:label` | Human-readable name of a resource |
| `rdfs:comment` | Human-readable description |

### 2.3 OWL Vocabulary — Core Constructs (High-Yield for KTU)

| OWL Construct | Mathematical Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `owl:Class` | $C$ | Defines a class of individuals |
| `owl:subClassOf` | $C_1 \sqsubseteq C_2$ | Class hierarchy |
| `owl:ObjectProperty` | $R$ | Binary relation between two individuals |
| `owl:DatatypeProperty` | $R$ | Relation from an individual to a literal |
| `owl:inverseOf` | $R_1 \equiv R_2^{-}$ | Two properties are mirror-images |
| `owl:TransitiveProperty` | $R \circ R \sqsubseteq R$ | If $aRb$ and $bRc$, then $aRc$ |
| `owl:SymmetricProperty` | $R \equiv R^{-}$ | If $aRb$, then $bRa$ |
| `owl:FunctionalProperty` | $\top \sqsubseteq \leq 1 R$ | Each subject maps to at most one object |
| `owl:InverseFunctionalProperty` | $\top \sqsubseteq \leq 1 R^{-}$ | Each object maps to at most one subject |
| `owl:cardinality` | $\vert R \cap \{x\} \vert = n$ | Exactly $n$ related resources |
| `owl:minCardinality` / `owl:maxCardinality` | bound limits | Numerical bound on relations |
| `owl:equivalentClass` | $C_1 \equiv C_2$ | Two classes have the same extension |
| `owl:sameAs` | $a \equiv b$ | Two individuals refer to the same entity |
| `owl:differentFrom` | $a \neq b$ | Two individuals are distinct |
| `owl:disjointWith` | $C_1 \sqcap C_2 \equiv \bot$ | Classes share no common members |
| `owl:Restriction` | $\exists R.C$, $\forall R.C$ | Existence/Universal value restriction |
| `owl:AllDifferent` | $a_i \neq a_j, \forall i \neq j$ | Pairwise distinct individuals |

### 2.4 OWL Sub-Languages Comparison (Always asked in KTU)

| Feature | OWL Lite | OWL DL | OWL Full |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expressiveness | Limited | High | Maximum |
| Decidability | Decidable | Decidable | **Not decidable** |
| Reasoning guarantee | Yes | Yes | No |
| Backward RDF compat | Partial | Partial | **Full** |
| Cardinality allowed | 0 or 1 only | 0,1,…,n | 0,1,…,n |
| Set of language | $\mathcal{ALIF(D)}$ | $\mathcal{SHOIN(D)}$ | Full RDF + OWL |

### 2.5 Why RDF and OWL Matter in Social Network Analysis

- **RDF** provides the *data layer* — store who knows whom (`<Person1> <knows> <Person2>`).
- **OWL** provides the *semantic layer* — declare that `knows` is symmetric, that `friend` is a sub-property of `knows`, that two nodes are the `sameAs` (identity resolution across Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn).
- The combination enables **semantic interoperability** between heterogeneous social platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The **FOAF (Friend of a Friend)** ontology and **SIOC (Semantically-Interlinked Online Communities)** project are both built on RDF + OWL. They power tools like Google Knowledge Graph and DBpedia.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Building an RDF Graph from a Sentence

**Example Sentence:** *"Arun, who is a Professor, teaches the course 'Social Network Analysis' at KTU."*

**Step 1 — Identify the URI prefixes.** Declare reusable namespaces to shorten URIs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prefix} \quad &\texttt{ex:} \rightarrow \texttt{http://university.example.org/} \\
&\texttt{rdf:} \rightarrow \texttt{http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns\#} \\
&\texttt{rdfs:} \rightarrow \texttt{http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema\#}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve every named entity into a URI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Arun} &\rightarrow \texttt{ex:Arun} \\
\text{Professor} &\rightarrow \texttt{ex:Professor} \\
\text{Social Network Analysis} &\rightarrow \texttt{ex:CourseSNA} \\
\text{KTU} &\rightarrow \texttt{ex:KTU} \\
\text{teaches} &\rightarrow \texttt{ex:teaches}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose the sentence into atomic triples.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \langle \texttt{ex:Arun}, \texttt{rdf:type}, \texttt{ex:Professor} \rangle \\
T_2 &= \langle \texttt{ex:Arun}, \texttt{ex:teaches}, \texttt{ex:CourseSNA} \rangle \\
T_3 &= \langle \texttt{ex:CourseSNA}, \texttt{rdf:type}, \texttt{ex:Course} \rangle \\
T_4 &= \langle \texttt{ex:Arun}, \texttt{ex:worksAt}, \texttt{ex:KTU} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Encode in Turtle (a common RDF syntax).**

```turtle
@prefix ex: <http://university.example.org/> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .

ex:Arun    rdf:type    ex:Professor .
ex:Arun    ex:teaches  ex:CourseSNA .
ex:CourseSNA  rdf:type    ex:Course .
ex:Arun    ex:worksAt  ex:KTU .
```

**Step 5 — Validate the triple structure.** Every predicate must be a URI, every subject must be a URI or blank node, and every object must be a URI, blank node, or literal.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: RDFS Reasoning — Class Hierarchy

**Axiom given:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{ex:Professor} \quad \texttt{rdfs:subClassOf} \quad \texttt{ex:Employee} \\
&\texttt{ex:Arun} \quad \texttt{rdf:type} \quad \texttt{ex:Professor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning Rule** (RDFS Entailment Rule rdfs9):
$$
\frac{A \xrightarrow{\text{subClassOf}} B \quad\quad C \xrightarrow{\text{type}} A}{C \xrightarrow{\text{type}} B}
$$

**Conclusion (Entailed Triple):**
$$
\texttt{ex:Arun} \quad \texttt{rdf:type} \quad \texttt{ex:Employee}
$$

This is **derivable by an OWL reasoner** like HermiT, Pellet, or FaCT++.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: OWL Inverse-Property Reasoning

**Axiom 1 (Inverse Declaration):**
$$
\texttt{ex:follows} \quad \texttt{owl:inverseOf} \quad \texttt{ex:followedBy}
$$

**Axiom 2 (Fact):**
$$
\texttt{ex:Alice} \quad \texttt{ex:follows} \quad \texttt{ex:Bob}
$$

**Inference Rule (InverseOf):**
$$
\frac{P \equiv Q^{-} \quad (a, P, b)}{(a, P, b) \text{ and } (b, Q, a)}
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\texttt{ex:Bob} \quad \texttt{ex:followedBy} \quad \texttt{ex:Alice}
$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: OWL Symmetric Property + Reasoning

**Axiom 1 (Symmetric Declaration):**
$$
\texttt{ex:knows} \quad \texttt{rdf:type} \quad \texttt{owl:SymmetricProperty}
$$

**Axiom 2 (Fact):**
$$
\texttt{ex:Arun} \quad \texttt{ex:knows} \quad \texttt{ex:Biju}
$$

**Inference Rule (Symmetricity):**
$$
\frac{P \equiv P^{-} \quad (a, P, b)}{(a, P, b) \text{ and } (b, P, a)}
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\texttt{ex:Biju} \quad \texttt{ex:knows} \quad \texttt{ex:Arun}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reasoning Capability vs Property

| OWL Property Axiom | Number of Explicit Triples Needed | Number of Triples a Reasoner Can Infer |
|---|---|---|
| Functional | 0 | 1 (uniqueness of image) |
| InverseFunctional | 0 | 1 (uniqueness of pre-image) |
| Transitive | 0 | $\binom{n}{2}$ chain links |
| Symmetric | 1 | 2 (forward + reverse) |
| InverseOf | 1 | 2 (forward + inverse) |
| subPropertyOf | 1 | $k$ (one for each super-property) |

### 3.6 Worked Example: SNA Identity Resolution (KTU Favourite)

**Scenario:** Two users appear as `ex:Arun_Kerala` on a forum and `ex:arun.k@iitb` on another. We want to merge them.

**OWL Axioms:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{ex:Arun\_Kerala} \quad \texttt{owl:sameAs} \quad \texttt{ex:arun.k@iitb} \\
&\texttt{ex:Arun\_Kerala} \quad \texttt{ex:teaches} \quad \texttt{ex:CourseSNA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inferred Triple:**
$$
\texttt{ex:arun.k@iitb} \quad \texttt{ex:teaches} \quad \texttt{ex:CourseSNA}
$$

This is the foundation of **entity resolution** in large SNA datasets.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: RDF Triple as Directed Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Subject: ex:Arun] -- "ex:teaches" --> O[Object: ex:CourseSNA]
    S -- "rdf:type" --> C1[Object: ex:Professor]
    O -- "rdf:type" --> C2[Object: ex:Course]
    S -- "ex:worksAt" --> W[Object: ex:KTU]
    C1 -- "rdfs:subClassOf" --> C3[Object: ex:Employee]
    C2 -- "rdfs:subClassOf" --> C4[Object: ex:AcademicUnit]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is a triple. Vertices are URIs. The two `rdf:type` arrows declare class membership; the `rdfs:subClassOf` edges declare taxonomy. The **blank node** is not represented here — but if we had an unknown subject, we would draw an unfilled circle.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Semantic Web Layer Cake (Most Asked in KTU)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Layer 1: Unicode + URI] --> L2[Layer 2: XML + Namespaces]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: RDF + RDFS]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: OWL Ontology]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: SPARQL Query]
    L4 --> L6[Layer 6: Logic + Proof]
    L5 --> L7[Layer 7: Trust]
    L6 --> L7
```

**Reading the diagram:** RDF is at the heart; OWL builds vocabularies on top of RDF; SPARQL allows querying; Logic enables inference; Trust enables verification. Each higher layer **uses** the lower layer.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: OWL Property Hierarchy (Description Logic View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[owl:ObjectProperty] --> Sym[owl:SymmetricProperty]
    Root --> Trans[owl:TransitiveProperty]
    Root --> Func[owl:FunctionalProperty]
    Root --> Inv[owl:InverseFunctionalProperty]
    Sym --> Ex1[ex:knows]
    Trans --> Ex2[ex:ancestorOf]
    Func --> Ex3[ex:hasBirthdate]
    Inv --> Ex4[ex:hasSSN]
    Sym --> Ex5[ex:friendOf]
    Ex1 -. owl:inverseOf .-> Ex1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: OWL Sub-Language Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Need to model an ontology?] --> B{Decidability required?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Need full cardinality?}
    C -- Only 0 or 1 --> D[Use OWL Lite]
    C -- Any number --> E[Use OWL DL]
    B -- No --> F[Use OWL Full]
    D --> G[Reasoner: FaCT++ Lite]
    E --> H[Reasoner: HermiT or Pellet]
    F --> I[Reasoner: None Full]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an RDF triple. What are the three components of a triple? Give one example.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An RDF triple is the atomic unit of RDF used to express a statement about a resource. It is a tuple $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ where:
> - **Subject (s)** — the resource being described (a URI or blank node)
> - **Predicate (p)** — the property or relationship (must be a URI)
> - **Object (o)** — the value of the property (URI, blank node, or literal)
>
> **Example:** $\langle \texttt{ex:Kerala}, \texttt{ex:isPartOf}, \texttt{ex:India} \rangle$ — meaning "Kerala is part of India." *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for components, 1 mark for example.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between RDFS and OWL in terms of expressiveness, with TWO examples of constructs unique to OWL.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - RDFS is a *lightweight* vocabulary that supports class hierarchies (`rdfs:subClassOf`) and property domains/ranges, but cannot express cardinality, symmetry, transitivity, or equivalence. *(1 mark)*
> - OWL is *much more expressive*, being based on Description Logic. It adds:
>   - `owl:SymmetricProperty` — to declare $P \equiv P^{-}$ *(1 mark)*
>   - `owl:equivalentClass` / `owl:sameAs` — to express identity and equality between classes/individuals *(1 mark)*
> - Hence OWL supports *reasoning* that RDFS cannot perform.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE — KTU Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the structure of the **RDF data model** with a neat diagram. Explain the role of URI, blank node, and literal with an example. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain any **FIVE core constructs of OWL** (with their mathematical meaning) and show how each can be used to model a *social network* where persons are friends, work in departments, and have unique email IDs. (7 marks)

---

> **Model Answer for Q3(a):**
> The **RDF data model** is a *directed, edge-labeled multigraph* in which every edge is a triple. (1 mark)
> - **URI (Uniform Resource Identifier):** A globally unique string used to identify *any* resource (both subject and object). Example: `<http://example.org/Arun>`. URIs are the *glue* that allows datasets to interlink. (2 marks)
> - **Blank Node:** An anonymous resource with no explicit URI; used to represent *reified* statements or composite values that have no inherent global name. Represented as `_:b1`. (2 marks)
> - **Literal:** A concrete data value (string, integer, date) — a leaf of the graph. Example: `"Arun"^^xsd:string`. (1 mark)
> - **Diagram (drawn):** Vertex for each URI/blank node, edge for each predicate, literal shown as a *rounded rectangle* attached by a label. (1 mark)

> **Model Answer for Q3(b):**
> Five OWL constructs and their SNA use:
> 1. `owl:SymmetricProperty` for `friendOf` — so that if $a$ is a friend of $b$, then $b$ is a friend of $a$. (2 marks)
> 2. `owl:InverseFunctionalProperty` for `hasEmailID` — two persons with the same email are the *same* individual. (2 marks)
> 3. `owl:TransitiveProperty` for `colleagueOf` (colleague-of-colleague is a colleague). (1 mark)
> 4. `owl:cardinality` = 1 for `hasBirthdate` — every person has exactly one birthdate. (1 mark)
> 5. `owl:disjointWith` between `Student` and `Faculty` — no overlap in role. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With **Turtle syntax examples**, explain the construction of an RDF graph for a *university knowledge base* having professors, courses, and departments. State the formal triple for each statement. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **OWL Lite, OWL DL, and OWL Full** along four dimensions. Justify why OWL DL is most commonly used in practice. (7 marks)

---

> **Model Answer for Q4(a):**
> A university knowledge base is built by issuing triples in Turtle (2 marks). Example:
> ```turtle
> @prefix ex: <http://univ.example.org/> .
> @prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
> @prefix owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> .
>
> ex:Arun       rdf:type        ex:Professor .
> ex:Arun       ex:teaches      ex:CourseSNA .
> ex:CourseSNA  rdf:type        ex:Course .
> ex:Arun       ex:worksIn      ex:DeptCSE .
> ex:DeptCSE    rdfs:label      "Computer Science" .
> ex:Arun       ex:hasFriend    ex:Biju .
> ex:Biju       ex:hasFriend    ex:Arun .  # could be inferred by OWL
> ```
> Formal triples: each Turtle line $\approx$ one $\langle s, p, o \rangle$. (3 marks)
> Note: $T_2 = \langle \texttt{ex:Arun}, \texttt{ex:hasFriend}, \texttt{ex:Biju} \rangle$, etc. (2 marks)

> **Model Answer for Q4(b):**
> Comparison along four dimensions (5 marks):
> | Dimension | OWL Lite | OWL DL | OWL Full |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Expressiveness | Lowest | High | Highest |
> | Decidability | Yes | Yes | No |
> | Cardinality | 0 or 1 only | 0, 1, …, n | 0, 1, …, n |
> | RDF compat | Partial | Partial | Full |
>
> **Why OWL DL is preferred:** (2 marks) It offers *maximum expressiveness without sacrificing decidability* — meaning a reasoner is *guaranteed to terminate* with a yes/no answer. OWL Lite is too weak; OWL Full is undecidable so reasoning may never end. Hence OWL DL is the sweet spot for SNA ontologies like FOAF and DBpedia.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students LOSE marks:**
> 1. ❌ Writing that **RDF is XML** — RDF is a *data model*; XML is one of several *syntaxes* (Turtle, N3, RDF/XML, JSON-LD are also valid). (–1 mark)
> 2. ❌ Confusing **RDF blank node** with **HTML comment** — blank nodes are anonymous resources, not documentation. (–1 mark)
> 3. ❌ Writing "OWL is a programming language" — OWL is a *declarative ontology language*, not procedural. (–1 mark)
> 4. ❌ Forgetting that **OWL DL is decidable** but OWL Full is not — this distinction is a 3-mark guaranteed question. (–2 marks)
> 5. ❌ Giving `sameAs` and `equivalentClass` the same meaning — the former is for *individuals*, the latter for *classes*. (–1 mark)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **RDF** = data model = *graph of triples*. (✓)
- **RDF Triple** = $\langle \text{Subject}, \text{Predicate}, \text{Object} \rangle$. (✓)
- **Subject & Predicate** must be URIs (or blank for subject). **Object** may be URI, blank, or literal. (✓)
- **RDFS** adds `subClassOf`, `subPropertyOf`, `domain`, `range`, `label`, `comment`. (✓)
- **OWL** is layered *on top of* RDFS and uses **Description Logic** semantics. (✓)
- **OWL Lite / DL / Full** — remember: Lite (simple), DL (decidable + expressive), Full (undecidable). (✓)
- **owl:sameAs** → individual equality; **owl:equivalentClass** → class equality. (✓)
- **owl:TransitiveProperty**, **owl:SymmetricProperty**, **owl:FunctionalProperty**, **owl:InverseFunctionalProperty** — the "big four" property axioms. (✓)
- **owl:cardinality** / **minCardinality** / **maxCardinality** → numerical constraints on relations. (✓)
- **FOAF** ontology is the canonical SNA use case of RDF + OWL. (✓)
- **SPARQL** is the query language for RDF graphs (analogous to SQL for relational DBs). (✓)
- **Decidability** = reasoning always terminates. (✓)
- **Description Logic** family names: $\mathcal{ALIF(D)}$ (Lite), $\mathcal{SHOIN(D)}$ (DL). (✓)
- RDF graph = labeled directed multigraph; **literals are leaves** (no outgoing edges). (✓)
- **Inference rules** you must know: rdfs9 (subClassOf + type $\Rightarrow$ type), Symmetricity, InverseOf, Transitivity. (✓)
- Use **Turtle** in KTU answers (clearer and more marks-friendly than RDF/XML). (✓)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data - State-of-the-art in Network Data Representation, Ontological Representation of Social Individuals, Ontological Representation of Social Relationships, Aggregating and Reasoning with Social Network Data, Advanced Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data - State-of-the-art in Network Data Representation, Ontological Representation of Social Individuals, Ontological Representation of Social Relationships, Aggregating and Reasoning with Social Network Data, Advanced Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — Modelling and Aggregating Social Network Data

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 State-of-the-Art in Network Data Representation

A **Social Network** in computer science is formally defined as a graph-theoretic construct $G = (V, E, L, f)$ where $V$ is a finite set of social actors (vertices), $E \subseteq V \times V$ represents the binary or weighted relational ties between actors, $L$ denotes a label alphabet, and $f: E \rightarrow L$ is a labelling function that maps each edge to a semantic descriptor. In modern SNA, this graph is enriched with **metadata, temporal stamps, and ontological annotations**, transforming it from a flat topological structure into a semantically meaningful knowledge substrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Network Data Representation* refers to the formal encoding scheme used to store, query, and exchange social network information. State-of-the-art representations move beyond simple adjacency matrices to **semantically-rich, machine-interpretable formats** such as RDF (Resource Description Framework), Property Graphs, and Ontological Knowledge Graphs.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of early social network data as a *photograph* — it captures who is connected to whom at a single frozen instant but tells you nothing about *what* the connection means (a friendship? a professional mentorship? a family tie?). Modern representation is more like a *high-definition video with subtitles and annotations* — every connection carries a timestamp, a meaning, a context, and a rating. The photograph is the adjacency matrix; the annotated video is the ontology-enriched knowledge graph.

**Three Generations of Network Representation:**

| Generation | Era | Representation | Limitation |
|------------|-----|----------------|------------|
| **G1 — Flat Graphs** | 1960s–1990s | Adjacency Matrix, Edge Lists | No semantics, no schema |
| **G2 — Relational DBs** | 1990s–2000s | Tables, Foreign Keys | Schema rigidity, joins expensive |
| **G3 — Semantic Graphs** | 2000s–Present | RDF, OWL, Property Graphs, Knowledge Graphs | Computational complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasises the shift from **syntactic** representation (what it *looks like*) to **semantic + ontological** representation (what it *means*).

### 1.2 Ontological Representation of Social Individuals

An **Ontology** in information science is a formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualisation. From a KTU standpoint, an ontology for social individuals is a 5-tuple:

$$\mathcal{O} = \langle \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{I}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{X} \rangle$$

where $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of **Classes** (concepts like `Person`, `Organization`, `Agent`), $\mathcal{R}$ is the set of **Relations/Properties** (binary predicates like `knows`, `worksFor`), $\mathcal{I}$ is the set of **Individuals** (concrete instances like `Alice`, `Bob`), $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of **Axioms** (logical constraints), and $\mathcal{X}$ is the set of **Inference Rules**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are filling out a passport application. The form asks for your *Name, Date of Birth, Nationality, Address* — these are the **classes** (categories of data). Your specific entries — `Alice`, `1995-08-15`, `Indian`, `Kerala` — are the **individuals** (concrete values). The rule that *Date of Birth must be a valid calendar date* is an **axiom**. Together, the form is the **ontology** — a structured contract that any system can read and validate.

**Standard Ontologies for Social Individuals:**

1. **FOAF (Friend of a Friend):** The foundational ontology for representing people and their social connections.
2. **SIOC (Semantically-Interlinked Online Communities):** Represents users, posts, forums, and online interactions.
3. **Schema.org Person:** Lightweight vocabulary used by major search engines.
4. **vCard / VIAF:** Identity and authority records for persons.

### 1.3 Ontological Representation of Social Relationships

A **Social Relationship** in ontological terms is a **typed, directed, and constrained edge** in the knowledge graph, formally expressed as an RDF triple:

$$t = \langle s, p, o \rangle \in \mathcal{I} \times \mathcal{R} \times (\mathcal{I} \cup \mathcal{L})$$

where $s$ is the subject (source actor), $p$ is the predicate (relationship type), $o$ is the object (target actor or literal), and $\mathcal{L}$ is the set of literal values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Relationships are **first-class citizens** in ontological representations — they are not mere edges but carry their own properties, constraints, and inference rules.

**Conceptual Analogy:** If a social individual is a *character* in a story, then a social relationship is the *scripted scene* between two characters — complete with dialogue (the literal value of the relationship), stage directions (the metadata), and dramatic rules (e.g., "Betrayal can only occur if Trust ≥ threshold"). The ontology captures all of this.

**Core Properties of Ontological Relationships:**

| Property | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Symmetric** | $p(x,y) \Rightarrow p(y,x)$ | `knows`, `siblingOf` |
| **Transitive** | $p(x,y) \wedge p(y,z) \Rightarrow p(x,z)$ | `ancestorOf`, `colleagueOf` |
| **Functional** | $p(x,y) \wedge p(x,z) \Rightarrow y = z$ | `hasBiologicalMother` |
| **Inverse Functional** | $p(y,x) \wedge p(z,x) \Rightarrow y = z$ | `hasEmailAddress` |
| **Asymmetric** | $p(x,y) \Rightarrow \neg p(y,x)$ | `isParentOf` |

### 1.4 Aggregating and Reasoning with Social Network Data

**Aggregation** is the process of combining data from multiple heterogeneous social network sources (e.g., Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn) into a unified, coherent representation. **Reasoning** is the application of logical inference rules over the aggregated knowledge graph to derive new (implicit) facts from explicitly stated (explicit) facts.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a librarian receiving scattered index cards from different libraries worldwide — some in English, some in Chinese, some typed, some handwritten. The librarian's job is to:
1. **Aggregate** — translate them all into a single standard index format.
2. **Reason** — use the cross-references to deduce new entries (e.g., "If Author A wrote Book X, and Book X cites Book Y written by Author B, then Author A and Author B share a research field").

In computational terms:

$$\text{Knowledge Base} = \text{Aggregated Triples} \cup \text{Ontology Axioms}$$

$$\text{Inferred Knowledge} = \text{Reasoner}(\text{Knowledge Base})$$

### 1.5 Advanced Representations

These are **state-of-the-art (2024)** extensions that go beyond static semantic graphs:

- **Temporal Knowledge Graphs:** Edges carry timestamp validity intervals.
- **Heterogeneous Information Networks (HINs):** Multiple node and edge types.
- **Graph Embeddings:** Vector-space encodings of nodes/edges.
- **Hypergraphs:** Edges can connect any number of nodes (group relationships).
- **Probabilistic Soft Logic (PSL):** Uncertainty-aware relational reasoning.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically asks for awareness of these advanced representations as the frontier of the field.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 State-of-the-Art in Network Data Representation — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Evolution of Representation Schemes

**Step 1 — From Adjacency Matrices to Labeled Graphs:**
The classical adjacency matrix $A \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}$ (or weighted $A \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{n \times n}$) captures topology but discards semantics. The matrix entry $A_{ij} = w_{ij}$ encodes only the *strength* of the tie, not its *nature*.

**Step 2 — Introduction of Typed Edges:**
We introduce an edge-label function $\lambda: E \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ where $\Sigma$ is a vocabulary of relationship types (e.g., `friend`, `colleague`, `family`). This yields a **typed graph** or **multigraph**.

**Step 3 — Property Graphs:**
A property graph extends the typed graph by allowing *attributes on both nodes and edges*:

$$PG = \langle V, E, \text{prop}_V, \text{prop}_E, \lambda \rangle$$

with $\text{prop}_V: V \rightarrow \mathcal{K} \rightarrow \mathcal{L}$ and $\text{prop}_E: E \rightarrow \mathcal{K} \rightarrow \mathcal{L}$ where $\mathcal{K}$ is the set of property keys and $\mathcal{L}$ is the set of literal values.

**Step 4 — RDF Triple Model:**
The Resource Description Framework represents information as a set of triples:

$$\mathcal{T} = \{\langle s_i, p_i, o_i \rangle \mid 1 \leq i \leq m\}$$

Each triple is a directed, labelled edge in an *infinite, globally addressable* graph (the "Semantic Web"). The subject and predicate are URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers); the object may be a URI or a literal.

**Step 5 — Ontological Layer (RDFS + OWL):**
On top of RDF, we layer **RDF Schema (RDFS)** for class/property hierarchies and **Web Ontology Language (OWL)** for expressive logical axioms.

### 2.1.2 Comparative Analysis of Representation Schemes

| Feature | Adjacency Matrix | Property Graph | RDF | RDF + OWL |
|---------|----------------|---------------|-----|-----------|
| **Schema** | None | Implicit | RDFS optional | Explicit ontology |
| **Semantics** | None | Label keys | URI vocabulary | First-order logic |
| **Reasoning** | ❌ | ❌ | Basic RDFS | Full OWL DL |
| **Scalability** | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | High (graph DBs) | High (triple stores) | Moderate |
| **Standardisation** | Math | Vendor-specific (Neo4j, TinkerPop) | W3C standard | W3C standard |
| **Inference** | ❌ | Limited | `rdfs:subClassOf` | Full Description Logic |

### 2.2 Ontological Representation of Social Individuals — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.2.1 The FOAF Ontology — Deep Dive

**FOAF** (Friend of a Friend) was proposed by Dan Brickley and Libby Miller in 2000 and remains the de-facto standard for representing individuals. Its core class hierarchy is:

$$\text{foaf:Person} \sqsubseteq \text{foof:Agent}$$
$$\text{foaf:Agent} \sqsubseteq \text{owl:Thing}$$

**Core FOAF Properties for an Individual:**

| Property | Domain | Range | Inverse | Inverse Of |
|----------|--------|-------|---------|------------|
| `foaf:name` | `foaf:Person` | `rdfs:Literal` | — | — |
| `foaf:mbox` | `foaf:Person` | `foaf:mbox` | — | — |
| `foaf:knows` | `foaf:Person` | `foaf:Person` | symmetric | — |
| `foaf:based_near` | `foaf:Person` | `foaf:Document` | — | — |
| `foaf:age` | `foaf:Person` | `xsd:integer` | functional | — |
| `foaf:gender` | `foaf:Person` | `xsd:string` | — | — |
| `foaf:homepage` | `foaf:Person` | `foaf:Document` | — | — |
| `foaf:depiction` | `foaf:Person` | `foaf:Image` | — | — |
| `foaf:familyName` | `foaf:Person` | `xsd:string` | — | — |
| `foaf:givenName` | `foaf:Person` | `xsd:string` | — | — |

**Axiomatic Definition of `knows` in OWL 2:**

$$\text{SymmetricObjectProperty(foaf:knows)} \equiv \text{ObjectProperty(foaf:knows)} \sqcap \forall x,y \; (\text{knows}(x,y) \rightarrow \text{knows}(y,x))$$

### 2.2.2 SIOC Ontology — Online Communities

The SIOC (Semantically-Interlinked Online Communities) ontology models:

$$\text{sioc:User} \sqsubseteq \text{foaf:OnlineAccount} \sqsubseteq \text{foaf:Agent}$$

Core SIOC classes:
- `sioc:User` — a person in an online community.
- `sioc:Post` — a message/article.
- `sioc:Forum` — a discussion space.
- `sioc:Thread` — a sequence of related posts.
- `sioc:Community` — a group of users.
- `sioc:Site` — a hosting platform.

### 2.2.3 Identity, Reification, and Trust

When representing an individual, we must address three subtle issues:

1. **Identity Uniqueness:** Using `foaf:mbox_sha1sum` (inverse functional) guarantees global uniqueness.
2. **Reification:** When we need to *talk about* a fact (e.g., "Alice says she knows Bob"), we reify the triple as a resource: $r = \langle \text{about}, s, \text{reifyStatement} \rangle$.
3. **Trust:** Statements can be qualified using Named Graphs or PROV-O (provenance ontology).

### 2.3 Ontological Representation of Social Relationships — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Relationship Typology

Social relationships can be taxonomised into the following orthogonal dimensions:

**Dimension 1 — Sign:**
$$\text{sign}(p) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } p \text{ is positive (friendship, trust)} \\ -1 & \text{if } p \text{ is negative (conflict, distrust)} \\ 0 & \text{if } p \text{ is neutral} \end{cases}$$

**Dimension 2 — Strength:**
$$w: E \rightarrow [0,1] \quad \text{where } w_{ij} = \frac{\vert \text{interactions}(i,j) \vert}{\max_{k,l} \text{interactions}(k,l)}$$

**Dimension 3 — Polarity:**
- **Directed:** `p(i,j)` does not imply `p(j,i)` (e.g., `isParentOf`).
- **Undirected:** Equivalent in both directions (e.g., `knows`).
- **Bidirectional Directed:** Both `p(i,j)` and `p(j,i)` may exist independently (e.g., `likes`).

**Dimension 4 — Cardinality:**
$$\text{card}(p) = \sup \vert \{ o \mid p(s,o) \} \vert \text{ for a fixed } s$$

### 2.3.2 The Relationship Property Matrix (OWL 2)

| OWL 2 Property Characteristic | Mathematical Form | Social Example |
|------------------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| `TransitiveProperty` | $\forall x,y,z: p(x,y) \wedge p(y,z) \rightarrow p(x,z)$ | `isAncestorOf` |
| `SymmetricProperty` | $\forall x,y: p(x,y) \rightarrow p(y,x)$ | `knows`, `isSiblingOf` |
| `AsymmetricProperty` | $\forall x,y: p(x,y) \rightarrow \neg p(y,x)$ | `isParentOf` |
| `ReflexiveProperty` | $\forall x: p(x,x)$ | `knows` (in some models) |
| `IrreflexiveProperty` | $\forall x: \neg p(x,x)$ | `isParentOf` |
| `FunctionalProperty` | $\forall x,y,z: p(x,y) \wedge p(x,z) \rightarrow y = z$ | `hasBiologicalMother` |
| `InverseFunctionalProperty` | $\forall x,y,z: p(y,x) \wedge p(z,x) \rightarrow y = z$ | `hasEmailAddress` |

### 2.3.3 Inference Example

Consider the FOAF statements:
- `<Alice> foaf:knows <Bob>`
- `<Bob> foaf:knows <Charlie>`
- `foaf:knows rdf:type owl:TransitiveProperty`

**Reasoner output (using RDFS/OWL semantics):**
$$\langle \text{Alice, foaf:knows, Charlie} \rangle \quad \text{(inferred)}$$

This is an example of **materialised inference** — implicit knowledge is derived from explicit axioms.

### 2.4 Aggregating and Reasoning — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.4.1 The Aggregation Pipeline

The canonical pipeline for aggregating social network data is:

$$\text{Stage 1: Extraction} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2: Transformation} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3: Mapping} \rightarrow \text{Stage 4: Fusion} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5: Validation}$$

**Stage 1 — Extraction:** Data pulled from APIs (Twitter v2, Graph API, LinkedIn) and web scraping.

**Stage 2 — Transformation:** Convert to canonical RDF format using tools like **D2RQ** (relational-to-RDF) or **RML** (RDF Mapping Language).

**Stage 3 — Mapping:** Align to a target ontology using **ontology matching** algorithms:
- **String-based:** Edit distance, Jaccard similarity on labels.
- **Structure-based:** Graph isomorphism on class hierarchies.
- **Logic-based:** SAT solvers / DL reasoners (e.g., AgreementMaker, COMA).

**Stage 4 — Fusion:** Resolve duplicates using **inverse functional properties** (e.g., same email = same person).

**Stage 5 — Validation:** Check consistency with a reasoner (Pellet, HermiT, FaCT++).

### 2.4.2 Reasoning Tasks

A **Reasoner** (or inference engine) takes a Knowledge Base $KB = \langle TBox, ABox \rangle$ and performs tasks:

| Task | Definition | Algorithm |
|------|------------|-----------|
| **Subsumption** | Is $C_1 \sqsubseteq C_2$? | Tableau-based |
| **Instance Checking** | Is $a \in C$? | Tableau |
| **Consistency** | Is $KB$ satisfiable? | Tableau / SAT |
| **Realisation** | Most specific class of $a$? | Tableau |
| **Retrieval** | All $a$ such that $C(a)$? | Backward-chaining |

### 2.4.3 SPARQL Query Pattern

The standard query language for RDF data is **SPARQL** (Protocol and RDF Query Language):

$$\text{SELECT } ?x \text{ WHERE } \{ ?x \text{ foaf:knows } ?y . \; ?y \text{ foaf:based\_near } \text{:Kerala} \}$$

This pattern asks for all individuals `x` who know someone `y` based in Kerala.

### 2.5 Advanced Representations — Theoretical Deep Dive

### 2.5.1 Temporal Knowledge Graphs

A temporal KG introduces a time dimension to triples:

$$t = \langle s, p, o, [\tau_{\text{start}}, \tau_{\text{end}}] \rangle$$

Validity is restricted to the time interval. Common models: **TimeML**, **OWL-Time**, **4D fluents**.

### 2.5.2 Knowledge Graph Embeddings

Embeddings map entities and relations into a continuous vector space $\mathbb{R}^d$. For a triple $\langle h, r, t \rangle$, the goal is:

$$f(h, r, t) \approx 0 \quad \text{if true}, \quad f(h, r, t) \gg 0 \quad \text{if false}$$

| Model | Scoring Function $f(h, r, t)$ |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| **TransE** | $\Vert \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t} \Vert_{L_1/L_2}$ |
| **TransR** | $\Vert M_r \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - M_r \mathbf{t} \Vert$ |
| **DistMult** | $\langle \mathbf{h}, \mathbf{r}, \mathbf{t} \rangle = \sum_i h_i r_i t_i$ |
| **ComplEx** | $\text{Re}(\langle \mathbf{h}, \mathbf{r}, \bar{\mathbf{t}} \rangle)$ in $\mathbb{C}^d$ |
| **RotatE** | $\Vert \mathbf{h} \circ \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t} \Vert$ with $\mathbf{r}_i = e^{i\theta_i}$ |

### 2.5.3 Heterogeneous Information Networks (HINs)

A HIN is a directed graph with a **node-type mapping** $\phi_V: V \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ and **edge-type mapping** $\phi_E: E \rightarrow \mathcal{R}$, where $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert + \vert \mathcal{R} \vert > 2$.

In social networks: $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Person, Post, Group, Event, Location}\}$, $\mathcal{R} = \{\text{posted, liked, joined, attended, locatedAt}\}$.

### 2.5.4 Hypergraphs

A hypergraph generalises edges to **hyperedges** that connect any subset of vertices:

$$\mathcal{H} = (V, \mathcal{E}), \quad \mathcal{E} \subseteq 2^V$$

For social networks, hyperedges naturally model *group memberships* — e.g., a WhatsApp group is a hyperedge containing all members.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Domain / Constraint |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Graph Model | $G = (V, E, L, f)$ | $E \subseteq V \times V$ |
| 2 | RDF Triple | $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ | $s, p \in \text{URI}; \; o \in \text{URI} \cup \text{Literal}$ |
| 3 | Ontology 5-tuple | $\mathcal{O} = \langle \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{I}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{X} \rangle$ | $\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{X}$ are logical formulae |
| 4 | Symmetric Property | $p(x,y) \Leftrightarrow p(y,x)$ | `knows`, `siblingOf` |
| 5 | Transitive Property | $p(x,y) \wedge p(y,z) \Rightarrow p(x,z)$ | `ancestorOf` |
| 6 | Functional Property | $\forall y,z: p(x,y) \wedge p(x,z) \Rightarrow y = z$ | `hasMother` |
| 7 | Inverse Functional | $\forall y,z: p(y,x) \wedge p(z,x) \Rightarrow y = z$ | `hasEmail` |
| 8 | Edge Strength Norm. | $w_{ij} = \frac{c_{ij}}{\max_{k,l} c_{kl}}$ | $w_{ij} \in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | TransE Score | $f(h,r,t) = \Vert \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t} \Vert_{L_2}$ | $\mathbf{h}, \mathbf{r}, \mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| 10 | KB Decomposition | $KB = TBox \cup ABox$ | $TBox$ = schema; $ABox$ = instances |
| 11 | Hyperedge | $\mathcal{E} \subseteq 2^V$ | $\vert \mathcal{E}_i \vert \geq 2$ |
| 12 | Reasoner Output | $KB \models \alpha$ | Entailment |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recommendation Systems:** Embeddings power Pinterest, Amazon, and LinkedIn "People You May Know".
- **Fraud Detection:** Reasoning engines detect inconsistencies in social identity graphs.
- **Public Health:** Aggregating Twitter + mobility data for epidemic forecasting.
- **Search Engines:** Google's Knowledge Graph (2012–present) is built on ontology-based representation.
- **Law Enforcement:** OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) tools aggregate social profiles into unified knowledge graphs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Plain Graph to RDF Triple Set

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: A Simple Social Graph

Consider three individuals: **Alice**, **Bob**, **Charlie** with the following relationships:
- Alice knows Bob
- Bob knows Charlie
- Alice is 28 years old
- Bob is based in Kerala

Represented as an edge list and property table:

$$V = \{\text{Alice, Bob, Charlie}\}$$
$$E = \{\langle \text{Alice, knows, Bob} \rangle, \langle \text{Bob, knows, Charlie} \rangle\}$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Transformation to RDF

**Step 1 — Assign URIs to Individuals.**

We mint globally unique URIs (typically using the namespace of the data publisher):

$$a = \text{:Alice}, \quad b = \text{:Bob}, \quad c = \text{:Charlie}$$

**Step 2 — Use standard vocabulary for predicates.**

We employ the **FOAF** vocabulary namespace `http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/` and abbreviate as `foaf:`. Age uses `xml:http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#integer` (abbreviated `xsd:integer`).

**Step 3 — Construct explicit triples.**

Each fact becomes one triple:

$$t_1 = \langle :\text{Alice, foaf:knows, :Bob} \rangle$$
$$t_2 = \langle :\text{Bob, foaf:knows, :Charlie} \rangle$$
$$t_3 = \langle :\text{Alice, foaf:age, "28" \rangle$$
$$t_4 = \langle :\text{Bob, foaf:based\_near, :Kerala} \rangle$$

**Step 4 — Add the ontology layer (TBox).**

To enable reasoning, we declare the schema:

$$t_5 = \langle \text{foaf:Person, rdf:type, rdfs:Class} \rangle$$
$$t_6 = \langle \text{foaf:knows, rdf:type, owl:SymmetricProperty} \rangle$$
$$t_7 = \langle \text{foaf:knows, rdf:type, owl:TransitiveProperty} \rangle$$
$$t_8 = \langle \text{foaf:age, rdf:type, owl:DatatypeProperty} \rangle$$
$$t_9 = \langle \text{foaf:age, rdf:type, owl:FunctionalProperty} \rangle$$

**Step 5 — Assert typing of individuals.**

$$t_{10} = \langle :\text{Alice, rdf:type, foaf:Person} \rangle$$
$$t_{11} = \langle :\text{Bob, rdf:type, foaf:Person} \rangle$$
$$t_{12} = \langle :\text{Charlie, rdf:type, foaf:Person} \rangle$$

**Step 6 — Full knowledge base:**

$$\boxed{KB = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5, t_6, t_7, t_8, t_9, t_{10}, t_{11}, t_{12}\}}$$

### 3.1.3 Explicit Reasoning Derivation

**Input:** $KB$ as above.

**Derivation Step 1 — Apply Symmetry of `foaf:knows`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
& \text{Given: } :\text{Alice, foaf:knows, :Bob} \quad (\text{from } t_1) \\
& \text{Axiom: SymmetricProperty(foaf:knows)} \\
& \text{Conclusion: } :\text{Bob, foaf:knows, :Alice} \quad (\text{inferred } t_{13})
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation Step 2 — Apply Transitivity of `foaf:knows`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
& \text{Given: } :\text{Alice, foaf:knows, :Bob} \quad (\text{from } t_1) \\
& \text{Given: } :\text{Bob, foaf:knows, :Charlie} \quad (\text{from } t_2) \\
& \text{Axiom: TransitiveProperty(foaf:knows)} \\
& \text{Conclusion: } :\text{Alice, foaf:knows, :Charlie} \quad (\text{inferred } t_{14})
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Combine with symmetry:**

From $t_{14}$ and the symmetry axiom:

$$\text{Inferred: } :\text{Charlie, foaf:knows, :Alice} \quad (t_{15})$$

**Final Entailed Triples:**

$$KB \models \{t_{13}, t_{14}, t_{15}\}$$

The original 12 triples expanded to 15 — a **25% increase in knowledge** with no additional data entry.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reasoning Complexity

Let $n = \vert \mathcal{T} \vert$ be the number of explicit triples and $m$ the number of ontology axioms. The complexity of common reasoning tasks is:

| Task | Complexity Class | Tableau Bound | Practical Optimisation |
|------|------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| KB Consistency (SHOIN) | **NEXPTIME-complete** | $O(2^{O(m)})$ | Pellet uses UCQ rewriting |
| Subsumption (EL++) | **PTIME** | $O(\text{polynomial})$ | Snorel reasoner |
| Instance Retrieval (SPARQL) | **PSPACE-complete** | $O(n \log n)$ | Triple-store indexing |
| Materialisation (RDFS) | **PTIME** | $O(n \cdot m)$ | Incremental computation |

## 3.3 Derivation — Knowledge Graph Embedding (TransE)

### 3.3.1 The Translation Principle

For a true triple $\langle h, r, t \rangle$, the **translation principle** states:

$$\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} \approx \mathbf{t}$$

The corresponding scoring function (which should be **minimised** for true triples):

$$f(h, r, t) = \Vert \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t} \Vert_{L_1/L_2}$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Example

Let $\mathbf{h}_{\text{Alice}} = (1, 0)$, $\mathbf{r}_{\text{knows}} = (0.5, 0.5)$, $\mathbf{t}_{\text{Bob}} = (1.5, 0.5)$.

**Compute the score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f(\text{Alice, knows, Bob}) &= \Vert (1, 0) + (0.5, 0.5) - (1.5, 0.5) \Vert_2 \\
&= \Vert (0, 0) \Vert_2 \\
&= 0 \quad \text{(ideal — perfectly predicted)}
\end{aligned}$$

Now check a false triple $\langle \text{Alice, knows, Charlie} \rangle$ with $\mathbf{t}_{\text{Charlie}} = (0.2, 0.8)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(\text{Alice, knows, Charlie}) &= \Vert (1, 0) + (0.5, 0.5) - (0.2, 0.8) \Vert_2 \\
&= \Vert (1.3, -0.3) \Vert_2 \\
&= \sqrt{1.69 + 0.09} \\
&= \sqrt{1.78} \\
&\approx 1.334
\end{aligned}$$

The high score (compared to 0) correctly indicates that Alice is *less likely* to know Charlie according to the embedding.

### 3.3.3 Training Objective

TransE is trained with a margin-based loss over positive and negative samples:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(h,r,t) \in \mathcal{S}^+} \sum_{(h',r',t') \in \mathcal{S}^-} \max\left(0, \gamma + f(h,r,t) - f(h',r',t')\right)$$

where $\gamma > 0$ is the margin hyperparameter, $\mathcal{S}^+$ is the set of true triples, and $\mathcal{S}^-$ is the set of *corrupted* (negative) triples generated by replacing either $h$ or $t$ with a random entity.

## 3.4 Derivation — Heterogeneous Network Meta-Path

### 3.4.1 Meta-Path Definition

A **meta-path** $\mathcal{P}$ in a HIN is a sequence of node and edge types:

$$\mathcal{P} = (A_1 \xrightarrow{R_1} A_2 \xrightarrow{R_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{R_l} A_{l+1})$$

For example, in a movie-rating HIN:
- $\mathcal{P}_1$: $\text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{watches}} \text{Movie} \xrightarrow{\text{stars}} \text{Actor}$
- $\mathcal{P}_2$: $\text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{friends}} \text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{watches}} \text{Movie}$

### 3.4.2 Path Count Metric

The count of instances of meta-path $\mathcal{P}$ between nodes $u$ and $v$ is:

$$C_{\mathcal{P}}(u, v) = \vert \{ (u, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_l, v) \mid \text{type}(x_i) = A_{i+1} \} \vert$$

This count is normalised to give a similarity score:

$$\text{PathSim}(u, v \mid \mathcal{P}) = \frac{2 \times C_{\mathcal{P}}(u, v)}{C_{\mathcal{P}}(u, u) + C_{\mathcal{P}}(v, v)}$$

### 3.4.3 Worked Example

HIN: Two users $U_1, U_2$, three movies $M_1, M_2, M_3$, and the meta-path $\mathcal{P} = \text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{watches}} \text{Movie} \xrightarrow{\text{watchedBy}} \text{User}$ (length 2).

Suppose:
- $U_1$ watches $M_1, M_2$.
- $U_2$ watches $M_1, M_2, M_3$.

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\mathcal{P}}(U_1, U_2) &= 2 \quad (M_1, M_2) \\
C_{\mathcal{P}}(U_1, U_1) &= 2 \quad (M_1, M_2) \\
C_{\mathcal{P}}(U_2, U_2) &= 3 \quad (M_1, M_2, M_3)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PathSim}(U_1, U_2 \mid \mathcal{P}) &= \frac{2 \times 2}{2 + 3} \\
&= \frac{4}{5} \\
&= 0.8
\end{aligned}$$

This high similarity correctly indicates that $U_1$ and $U_2$ share strong viewing preferences.

## 3.5 Derivation — Temporal Triple Validity

For a temporal triple $t = \langle s, p, o, [\tau_s, \tau_e] \rangle$, the **validity interval** is:

$$\text{valid}(t) = [\tau_s, \tau_e]$$

A query at time $\tau_q$ is satisfied iff:

$$\tau_s \leq \tau_q \leq \tau_e$$

**Temporal entailment** (transitivity over time):

$$\begin{aligned}
& \langle s, p, o, [\tau_1, \tau_2] \rangle \wedge \langle o, p, s', [\tau_2, \tau_3] \rangle \\
& \quad \Rightarrow \langle s, p, s', [\tau_2, \tau_2] \rangle \quad \text{(only at overlap point } \tau_2 \text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix — KTU Module 2

| Subtopic | Core Abstraction | Critical Operator | Practical Bottleneck |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Network Representation | $G \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$ | Triple extraction | URI disambiguation |
| Social Individual Ontology | $\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{I}$ | Class instantiation | Identity reconciliation |
| Social Relationship | $p \in \mathcal{R}$ | Property characterisation | Symmetry/Transitivity reasoning |
| Aggregation | $\bigcup_i \mathcal{T}_i \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$ | Schema mapping | Heterogeneity conflict |
| Reasoning | $KB \models \alpha$ | Tableau algorithm | Worst-case complexity |
| Advanced Representation | Embedding $\phi: V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$ | Distance metric | Cold-start problem |

```mermaid
    ADV --> PSL
    ADV --> MUL

    EMB --> TR
    EMB --> COM
    EMB --> ROT
    EMB --> D
    EMB --> N
```
