- $k_6 = 2$ (edges to 4, 5)

**Step 2: Total edges**

$$m = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2m = 14$$

**Step 3: Intra-community edge contribution**
- $C_1 = \{1,2,3\}$: edges (1,2), (2,3), (1,3) — 3 internal edges.
- $C_2 = \{4,5,6\}$: edges (4,5), (5,6), (4,6) — 3 internal edges.
- Total internal edges = 6 out of 7 → density = 6/7.

**Step 4: Sum the modularity terms (both directions counted)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{C_1} &= \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i,j \in C_1}\left[A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{14}\left[ (1 - \tfrac{2 \cdot 2}{14}) \cdot 2 + (1 - \tfrac{2 \cdot 3}{14}) \cdot 2 + (1 - \tfrac{3 \cdot 3}{14}) \cdot 2 \right] \quad \text{(i<j pairs and symmetric)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For pairs inside $C_1$ (counting both (i,j) and (j,i)):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edge 1-2: } & A=1,\ \tfrac{k_1 k_2}{14}=\tfrac{4}{14}=0.2857 \Rightarrow 0.7143 \text{ (both directions)} \\
\text{Edge 2-3: } & A=1,\ \tfrac{k_2 k_3}{14}=\tfrac{6}{14}=0.4286 \Rightarrow 0.5714 \text{ (both directions)} \\
\text{Edge 1-3: } & A=1,\ \tfrac{k_1 k_3}{14}=\tfrac{6}{14}=0.4286 \Rightarrow 0.5714 \text{ (both directions)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum for $C_1 = 2(0.7143 + 0.5714 + 0.5714) = 2(1.8571) = 3.7143$

For $C_2$ (by symmetry, all nodes have the same degree distribution as $C_1$ since $k_4=3, k_5=2, k_6=2$ and edges (4,5), (5,6), (4,6)):

Sum for $C_2 = 3.7143$

**Step 5: Combine**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2m}\left[3.7143 + 3.7143\right] = \frac{7.4286}{14} \approx 0.5306
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

$Q \approx 0.53$ indicates a **strong community structure** — the partition $\{1,2,3\}, \{4,5,6\}$ is well-defined with only one bridging edge (3–4) connecting the two triangles.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Slot B]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the **Louvain algorithm** for community detection in detail. Compare it with the Girvan–Newman algorithm on the basis of complexity, scalability, and output. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Clique Percolation Method (CPM)** for detecting overlapping communities. What is the role of parameter $k$? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Louvain Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithmic Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Place each node in its own community (singleton).
2. **Phase 1 — Local Greedy Optimization:** For each node $i$, evaluate the modularity gain $\Delta Q$ obtained by removing $i$ from its current community and placing it into the community of each of its neighbours. Move $i$ to the community with the **largest non-negative** $\Delta Q$. Repeat until no further positive gain is achievable.
3. **Phase 2 — Network Aggregation:** Build a new coarse-grained network where each community from Phase 1 becomes a single node. The weight of an edge between two new nodes equals the sum of weights of all edges between the corresponding original communities. Self-loops represent intra-community edge weights.
4. **Iteration:** Apply Phase 1 to the new aggregated network. Continue until no modularity improvement is observed.
5. **Output:** A multi-level hierarchical community structure with the top-level partition representing the final community assignment.

**Modularity Gain Formula:**

$$
\Delta Q = \frac{k_{i,in}}{m} - \gamma \frac{\Sigma_{tot} \cdot k_i}{2m^2}
$$

where $\gamma$ is a resolution parameter (default = 1).

**Comparison Table with Girvan–Newman:**

| Aspect | Louvain | Girvan–Newman |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(m^2 n)$ |
| Scalability | Millions of nodes | Thousands of nodes |
| Output Quality | Approximate optimum | Exact optimum at each level |
| Determinism | Non-deterministic | Deterministic |
| Use Case | Very large networks | Small/medium academic nets |

**Part (b) — Clique Percolation Method (7 marks):**

**Definition (Palla et al., 2005):**
Two $k$-cliques are considered **adjacent** if they share exactly $k-1$ nodes. A **$k$-clique community** is the **union of all $k$-cliques reachable from each other** through a chain of adjacent $k$-cliques. This union of overlapping cliques naturally produces **overlapping communities**.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Step 1:** Identify all maximal cliques of size $k$ in the graph using the **Bron–Kerbosch algorithm**.
2. **Step 2:** Construct a *clique-clique overlap matrix* $O$ where $O_{ij}$ = number of shared nodes between clique $i$ and clique $j$.
3. **Step 3:** Build a *clique graph* where two cliques are connected by an edge if $O_{ij} \geq k-1$.
4. **Step 4:** Extract connected components of the clique graph. Each connected component is a $k$-clique community.
5. **Step 5:** A node belongs to a community if it participates in at least one $k$-clique in the component.

**Role of $k$:**
- $k = 3$ (triangles) → broad, often large communities.
- $k = 4$ (tetrahedra) → tighter, smaller, more specific communities.
- $k = 2$ degenerates to connected components.
- **Higher $k$ ⇒ smaller, more cohesive communities; lower $k$ ⇒ larger, coarser communities.**

**Visual Sketch (textual block diagram):**

```
Triangle 1 — shares node 3 — Triangle 2 — shares node 5 — Triangle 3
   (1,2,3)                    (3,4,5)                    (5,6,7)
         |
     Community: {1,2,3,4,5,6,7} ← all reachable via k=3
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **edge betweenness** with **node betweenness** in GN. Edge betweenness counts paths *through edges*; node betweenness counts paths *through nodes*. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2m}$ in modularity formula and submitting only the sum. Modular value must be normalized. (Loses 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Treating CPM as a *non-overlapping* method. The whole point of CPM is **overlap** — explicitly state this in your answer. (Loses 1 mark.)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the time complexity of Girvan–Newman. Examiners allocate a dedicated sub-mark for algorithmic complexity analysis.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing **modularity $Q$** with **modularity matrix $B_{ij} = A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}$** — these are different mathematical objects.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Methods for Community Detection and Mining**

- [x] **Community** = dense intra-group + sparse inter-group edges.
- [x] **Modularity $Q$** uses the configuration model as null; range $[-0.5, 1]$; practical $[0.3, 0.7]$.
- [x] **Resolution limit** of modularity: cannot detect communities smaller than $\sim \sqrt{m}$.
- [x] **Girvan–Newman** uses **edge betweenness** removal; complexity $O(m^2 n)$; produces a **dendrogram**.
- [x] **Louvain** uses **modularity gain $\Delta Q$** local moves + network aggregation; complexity $O(n \log n)$.
- [x] **Kernighan–Lin** is a **graph bisection** algorithm requiring pre-specified $k$.
- [x] **Hierarchical** has two flavours: **agglomerative (bottom-up)** and **divisive (top-down)**.
- [x] **Spectral clustering** uses the **graph Laplacian $L = D - A$** and the $k$ smallest non-trivial eigenvectors.
- [x] **Label Propagation** (Raghavan et al.) is a near-linear $O(n+m)$ asynchronous algorithm using neighbour-label majority.
- [x] **CPM** detects **overlapping** communities via adjacent $k$-cliques; uses Bron–Kerbosch for clique enumeration.
- [x] **Conductance $\phi(S)$** is the ratio of cut to volume — lower is better.
- [x] **Evaluation:** Use **NMI**, **ARI**, or **modularity** for benchmark comparisons.
- [x] **Infomap** uses **information-theoretic compression** of random walks — excellent for flow networks.
- [x] **Walktrap** performs **short random walks** and merges nodes with similar walk profiles.
- [x] For **very large networks (>1M nodes)**, prefer **Louvain, Label Propagation, or Infomap**.
- [x] For **small/medium academic networks** where **quality > speed**, use **Girvan–Newman** or **spectral methods**.
- [x] For **overlapping communities**, use **CPM, SLPA, or LFM**.
- [x] **Always** mention time complexity in a 14-mark algorithm question — it carries 1–2 implicit marks.
- [x] **Community mining** ≠ community detection; mining includes **evolution, dynamics, and role analysis** of communities.
- [x] **Resolution parameter $\gamma$** in Louvain: $\gamma > 1$ → smaller communities; $\gamma < 1$ → larger communities.
- [x] **Modularity maximization is NP-hard** — hence Louvain's greedy heuristic approach is the practical standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Community Mining Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Community Mining Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Social Network Analysis (PECDT863)**, **Community Mining Algorithms** are a class of graph-theoretic and optimization-driven computational procedures designed to identify, extract, and characterize densely connected substructures—called **communities, clusters, or modules**—within a large-scale network. A *community* is formally defined as a subset of vertices $C \subseteq V$ such that the internal edge density within $C$ is significantly greater than the expected density of edges between $C$ and the rest of the network $G = (V, E)$.

The mathematical intuition behind community mining rests on the principle of **homophily** (similar nodes connect) and **structural equivalence** (nodes sharing many neighbors likely belong to the same group). The **modularity function** $Q$ is the most widely used quality metric for evaluating the strength of a discovered community partition:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)
$$

where $A_{ij}$ is the adjacency matrix entry, $k_i$ and $k_j$ are the degrees of nodes $i$ and $j$, $m$ is the total number of edges, $c_i$ denotes the community of node $i$, and $\delta(c_i, c_j) = 1$ if $i$ and $j$ are in the same community, else $0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 3, students must demonstrate the ability to **apply** community mining algorithms (e.g., Girvan-Newman, Louvain, Label Propagation, Clique Percolation) to **real-world problem domains** and justify algorithm choice based on network characteristics such as scale, density, overlap, and dynamicity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city's WhatsApp groups**. The city's population is 10 lakh people, but they don't all chat with each other randomly. They form tight clusters—*office colleagues* in one dense group, *college friends* in another, *family members* in a third, and *cricket teammates* in a fourth. These groups are **communities**—internal messaging is heavy, but cross-group messages are sparse.

Now, suppose a marketing agency wants to send a promotional message about a new cricket bat. They don't want to spam the whole city. They want to find the *cricket community* specifically. **Community Mining Algorithms** are the automated digital detectives that sift through millions of chat records and discover, *without being told*, "Hey, these 4,500 people are clearly a tightly connected cluster talking about cricket!"

The same logic applies to:
- Facebook finding groups you might join,
- Google identifying related websites for search ranking,
- Biologists identifying protein complexes in cells,
- Banks detecting fraud rings of collusive accounts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Community mining is not just an academic exercise—it is the **operational backbone** of personalization engines, fraud detection systems, biological pathway discovery, and large-scale recommender systems used in production today.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Community Mining

| Pillar | Description | Representative Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Divisive (Top-Down)** | Starts with the whole graph and progressively removes inter-community edges | Girvan-Newman |
| **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up)** | Starts with individual nodes and merges similar ones iteratively | Louvain, Hierarchical Clustering |
| **Label Propagation (Dynamic)** | Nodes adopt the most frequent label among their neighbors | Raghavan–Albert–Kumara (RAK) |

---

## 1.4 Why "Applications" Matter in KTU Module 3

KTU specifically separates *Algorithms* from *Applications of Algorithms* in the syllabus to ensure students grasp **transferable engineering knowledge**. Knowing *how* Girvan-Newman works is insufficient; the exam expects you to articulate **when, where, and why** each algorithm is deployed in industry, healthcare, cybersecurity, and biological research. The remaining sections build this applied competency layer-by-layer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Application Domains

Community mining algorithms find deployment across **eight major application domains**. Each domain imposes unique constraints on algorithm choice, scalability, and interpretability.

### Domain 1: Online Social Networks (OSNs)
Used by Facebook, Twitter/X, Instagram, LinkedIn for:
- **Friend/connection suggestion** — communities reveal latent social ties.
- **Feed ranking** — content from community members is weighted higher.
- **Community detection for group formation** — Facebook Groups, LinkedIn Circles.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Label Propagation (scalable to billions of nodes) or Louvain (modularity-optimized).

### Domain 2: Biological & Biomedical Networks
- **Protein–Protein Interaction (PPI) networks** — communities correspond to **functional modules** (e.g., ribosome assembly, DNA repair complex).
- **Gene co-expression networks** — communities identify **co-regulated gene sets**.
- **Disease–gene association** — overlapping communities reveal pleiotropic genes.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Clique Percolation Method (CPM), since biological modules often overlap.

### Domain 3: Recommendation Systems
- **Collaborative filtering** — users in the same community share similar preferences.
- **Item co-purchase networks** — communities represent cross-sell opportunities.
- **Cold-start mitigation** — new users/items are placed in inferred communities.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Spectral clustering (for embedding-based) or Louvain.

### Domain 4: Marketing & Customer Segmentation
- **Targeted advertising** — ad campaigns are scoped to detected communities.
- **Viral marketing** — influential nodes within communities act as seeders.
- **Brand community analysis** — identifying loyal customer clusters for retention.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Louvain (interpretable modularity) or Infomap (flow-based).

### Domain 5: Cybersecurity & Fraud Detection
- **Fraud rings** — collusive accounts form dense subgraphs with sparse external links.
- **Anomaly detection** — nodes whose community assignment shifts suddenly indicate compromise.
- **Botnet identification** — coordinated fake accounts cluster as near-cliques.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Edge Betweenness (Girvan-Newman) for small forensic graphs; Louvain for large network-scale detection.

### Domain 6: Epidemiology & Public Health
- **Disease transmission networks** — communities correspond to **outbreak clusters**.
- **Contact tracing** — communities in physical proximity graphs (e.g., Wi-Fi/Bluetooth logs) help isolate hot-spots.
- **Vaccination prioritization** — high-betweenness bridges in communities are super-spreaders.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Spatio-temporal community detection (variants of Louvain + time decay).

### Domain 7: Knowledge Graphs & Information Retrieval
- **Topic clustering** — related concepts form communities in co-citation networks.
- **Search engine result grouping** — Google uses community-like structures to deduplicate results.
- **Semantic web** — communities in ontologies reflect conceptual hierarchies.
- **Algorithm of choice:** Spectral methods (for embedding-based KG clustering).

### Domain 8: Transportation, Urban Planning & IoT
- **Traffic flow analysis** — communities reveal commuting corridors.
- **Smart grid stability** — communities in power grids identify cascading failure zones.
- **Sensor network clustering** — communities guide efficient data aggregation in IoT.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Metric | Formula / Definition | Use Case / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Modularity $Q$** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left[ A_{ij} - \dfrac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$ | Quality measure for community partitions; $Q \in [-0.5, 1]$ |
| **Internal Density $\delta_{int}(C)$** | $\delta_{int}(C) = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert E_{in}(C) \vert}{\vert C \vert (\vert C \vert - 1)}$ | Measures tightness of a community |
| **Edge Betweenness $C_B(e)$** | $C_B(e) = \sum_{s \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | Used in Girvan-Newman to rank inter-community bridges |
| **Conductance $\phi(C)$** | $\phi(C) = \dfrac{\text{cut}(C, \bar{C})}{\min(\text{vol}(C), \text{vol}(\bar{C}))}$ | Spectral clustering objective; lower is better |
| **Normalized Cut (Ncut)** | $\text{Ncut}(A, B) = \dfrac{\text{cut}(A, B)}{\text{assoc}(A, V)} + \dfrac{\text{cut}(A, B)}{\text{assoc}(B, V)}$ | Used in image & web graph segmentation |
| **Jaccard Similarity $J(u,v)$** | $J(u,v) = \dfrac{\vert N(u) \cap N(v) \vert}{\vert N(u) \cup N(v) \vert}$ | Used to merge similar nodes in agglomerative algorithms |
| **Louvain Modularity Gain $\Delta Q$** | $\Delta Q = \left[ \dfrac{\Sigma_{in} + k_{i,in}}{2m} - \left( \dfrac{\Sigma_{tot} + k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ \dfrac{\Sigma_{in}}{2m} - \left( \dfrac{\Sigma_{tot}}{2m} \right)^2 - \left( \dfrac{k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right]$ | Decides node movement between communities |
| **Clique Percolation $k$-clique** | Overlap iff two $k$-cliques share $k-1$ nodes | Captures **overlapping** communities |
| **Label Propagation Asynchronous Update** | $c_i(t+1) = \arg\max_c \sum_{j \in N(i)} \delta(c_j(t), c)$ | Near-linear time $O(m)$ community discovery |
| **Infomap Map Equation $L(M)$** | $L(M) = q_\curvearrowright H(Q) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{\circlearrowright}^i H(P^i)$ | Information-theoretic community detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **modularity maximization** with **graph partitioning**. They are related but distinct—modularity does NOT require the number/size of communities to be pre-specified, whereas partitioning does. Always clarify this in exam answers.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Community Mining

The real-world utility of community mining extends beyond pure computer science:

1. **Telecommunications:** Identifying clusters of cell tower usage to optimize handover protocols and reduce dropped calls.
2. **Supply Chain Management:** Communities in supplier-buyer networks expose **single points of failure**.
3. **Political Science:** Detecting echo chambers and polarization in election discourse networks.
4. **Climate Science:** Communities in atmospheric measurement station networks reveal micro-climatic zones.
5. **Education:** Mining learning interaction networks (MOOCs) to identify at-risk student clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Deployment Scale:** Facebook's community detection pipeline processes **>2 billion users and >1 trillion edges** using distributed Louvain variants on the Giraph/Apache GraphX framework. Twitter's real-time trend clustering uses streaming label propagation on Apache Flink.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Application-Driven Metrics

### Derivation 1: Modularity Function $Q$ from First Principles

The modularity function measures the **fraction of edges within communities minus the expected fraction if edges were distributed randomly**, given the degree sequence.

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } e_{uv} &= \text{number of edges between community } u \text{ and community } v \\
\text{Total edges } m &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} \\
\text{Probability of an edge between } i \text{ and } j \text{ under null model} &= \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \\
\text{Modularity } Q &= \sum_{c} \left[ e_{cc} - \left( \frac{K_c}{2m} \right)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_c = \sum_{i \in c} k_i$ is the sum of degrees inside community $c$.

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**
- The adjacency entry $A_{ij} = 1$ if an edge exists between $i$ and $j$ (else $0$).
- Under the **configuration null model**, edges are rewired randomly while preserving the degree of each node. The expected number of edges between $i$ and $j$ becomes $\frac{k_i k_j}{2m}$.
- The term in brackets measures **deviation from randomness**.
- $\delta(c_i, c_j)$ restricts summation to intra-community pairs.
- The prefactor $\frac{1}{2m}$ normalizes $Q$ to the range $[-0.5, 1]$.
- **Higher $Q$** indicates a stronger community structure; empirically, $Q > 0.3$ is considered significant.

---

### Derivation 2: Edge Betweenness Centrality for Girvan-Newman

Edge betweenness $C_B(e)$ is the number of shortest paths between all pairs of vertices that pass through edge $e$.

$$
C_B(e) = \sum_{s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{st} &= \text{total number of shortest paths from } s \text{ to } t \\
\sigma_{st}(e) &= \text{number of such paths that traverse edge } e \\
C_B(e) &= \sum_{s \in V} \sum_{t \in V, t \neq s} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- Compute all-pairs shortest paths using **Brandes' algorithm** in $O(nm)$ time.
- For each edge $e = (u,v)$, count how many shortest paths use it.
- Normalize against total shortest paths between $s$ and $t$.
- Sum over all source–destination pairs.
- **Application insight:** In fraud detection, edges with the *highest* betweenness are typically bridges between fraud rings. Removing them splits collusive clusters cleanly.

---

### Derivation 3: Louvain Modularity Gain $\Delta Q$

When moving an isolated node $i$ into the community of neighbor $j$:

$$
\Delta Q = \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in} + 2k_{i,in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot} + k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot}}{2m} \right)^2 - \left( \frac{k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \Sigma_{in} &= \sum \text{edges inside community } c \\
\Sigma_{tot} &= \sum \text{edges incident to community } c \text{ (including external)} \\
k_i &= \text{degree of node } i \\
k_{i,in} &= \sum_{j \in c} A_{ij} = \text{edges from } i \text{ into } c \\
\text{New modularity} &= \frac{\Sigma_{in} + 2k_{i,in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot} + k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \\
\text{Old modularity} &= \frac{\Sigma_{in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot}}{2m} \right)^2 - \left( \frac{k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \\
\Delta Q &= \text{New} - \text{Old}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- The factor $2k_{i,in}$ accounts for $k_{i,in}$ edges entering $c$ *and* $k_{i,in}$ edges leaving $i$ (which were external but now become internal if $i$ joins $c$).
- Squared degree terms come from $\left(\frac{K_c}{2m}\right)^2$ in the modularity definition.
- $\Delta Q > 0$ means the move improves community quality — accept it.
- **Application insight:** This greedy local optimization is what makes Louvain **near-linear in practice**, enabling community mining on billion-edge graphs in seconds.

---

### Derivation 4: Jaccard Similarity Agglomerative Merging

For merging the two most similar communities $C_a$ and $C_b$:

$$
J(C_a, C_b) = \frac{\vert E(C_a, C_b) \vert}{\vert E(C_a, \cdot) \vert + \vert E(\cdot, C_b) \vert - \vert E(C_a, C_b) \vert}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(C_a, C_b) &= \frac{\text{Edges between } C_a \text{ and } C_b}{\text{Total edges incident to } C_a \text{ or } C_b} \\
\text{Numerator} &= \vert E(C_a, C_b) \vert \text{ (shared boundary)} \\
\text{Denominator} &= \vert E(C_a, \cdot) \vert + \vert E(\cdot, C_b) \vert - \vert E(C_a, C_b) \vert \text{ (union of incident edges)} \\
J(C_a, C_b) &\in [0, 1]; \quad J = 1 \iff C_a, C_b \text{ form a complete clique}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- Numerator captures direct inter-community ties.
- Denominator removes double-counting of boundary edges.
- $J$ near $1$ means strong candidate for merger; $J$ near $0$ means $C_a$ and $C_b$ are isolated.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Algorithm vs. Application

| Application | Network Size | Density | Overlap? | Dynamic? | **Best Algorithm** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Facebook friend suggestion | Billions | Sparse | Yes | Yes | Label Propagation | Linear scalability, handles streaming |
| PPI / Gene modules | $10^3$–$10^5$ | Moderate | Yes | No | Clique Percolation (CPM) | Captures overlapping functional modules |
| Fraud ring detection | $10^4$–$10^6$ | Dense in ring | No | No | Girvan-Newman | Edge betweenness exposes ring boundaries |
| Customer segmentation | $10^5$–$10^7$ | Sparse | No | No | Louvain | Best modularity, fast, interpretable |
| Disease outbreak tracing | $10^3$–$10^6$ | Temporal | Yes | Yes | Spatio-temporal Louvain | Time-aware modularity |
| Knowledge graph clustering | $10^6$–$10^8$ | Moderate | Yes | No | Spectral + Embedding | Leverages latent semantics |
| Smart grid stability | $10^4$–$10^5$ | Sparse | No | No | Edge Betweenness | Bridges reveal cascading paths |
| Botnet detection | $10^6$–$10^9$ | Very sparse | No | Yes | Streaming Louvain / DLP | Near-real-time flagging |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Modularity Calculation

Consider a graph with $m = 6$ edges, partitioned into 2 communities:
- Community $C_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with internal edges = 3 (triangle 1-2, 2-3, 1-3)
- Community $C_2 = \{4, 5, 6\}$ with internal edges = 2 (edge 4-5, 5-6)
- Inter-community edges = 1 (edge 3-4)

**Degrees:** $k_1 = k_2 = k_3 = 3$, $k_4 = k_5 = k_6 = 2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma_{in,1} &= 3, \quad K_{C_1} = 3+3+3 = 9 \\
\Sigma_{in,2} &= 2, \quad K_{C_2} = 2+2+2 = 6 \\
Q &= \left( \frac{3}{6} - \left( \frac{9}{12} \right)^2 \right) + \left( \frac{2}{6} - \left( \frac{6}{12} \right)^2 \right) \\
&= (0.5 - 0.5625) + (0.3333 - 0.25) \\
&= -0.0625 + 0.0833 \\
Q &= 0.0208
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $Q \approx 0.02$ is very low, indicating weak community structure. The graph is close to random. This result matches the intuition that the partition barely separates the two halves because of the strong 3-4 bridge.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Community Mining Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Network Data Source] --> B[Data Preprocessing Module]
    B --> C[Graph Construction Layer]
    C --> D[Algorithm Selection Engine]
    D --> E1[Girvan-Newman Engine]
    D --> E2[Louvain Engine]
    D --> E3[Label Propagation Engine]
    D --> E4[Clique Percolation Engine]
    D --> E5[Spectral Clustering Engine]
    E1 --> F[Community Partition Output]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Quality Evaluation Module]
    G --> G1[Modularity Q Score]
    G --> G2[Conductance Score]
    G --> G3[NMI / ARI vs Ground Truth]
    G --> H{Threshold Met?}
    H --> I[Yes - Deploy to Application Layer]
    H --> J[No - Tune Parameters]
    J --> D
    I --> K1[Recommendation System]
    I --> K2[Fraud Detection]
    I --> K3[Targeted Marketing]
    I --> K4[Biological Module Discovery]
    I --> K5[Epidemic Outbreak Tracing]
```

## 4.2 Application-to-Algorithm Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph App1[Social Media Apps]
        A1[Facebook Groups]
        A2[Twitter Trends]
        A3[LinkedIn Connections]
    end
    subgraph App2[Biology Apps]
        B1[Protein Complex Detection]
        B2[Gene Co-regulation]
    end
    subgraph App3[Security Apps]
        C1[Fraud Ring Mining]
        C2[Botnet Detection]
    end
    subgraph App4[Business Apps]
        D1[Customer Segmentation]
        D2[Recommendation Engine]
    end
    subgraph Algo[Algorithm Layer]
        AL1[Girvan-Newman]
        AL2[Louvain]
        AL3[Label Propagation]
        AL4[Clique Percolation]
    end
    A1 --> AL3
    A2 --> AL3
    A3 --> AL2
    B1 --> AL4
    B2 --> AL2
    C1 --> AL1
    C2 --> AL3
    D1 --> AL2
    D2 --> AL2
    AL1 --> R[Detected Communities]
    AL2 --> R
    AL3 --> R
    AL4 --> R
```

## 4.3 Louvain Two-Phase Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Local Modularity Optimization] --> Q1{Any node move increases Q?}
    Q1 --> P2[Yes - Move node to neighboring community]
    Q2 --> P3[No - Phase 1 done]
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> P4[Phase 2: Graph Aggregation]
    P4 --> P5[Build new graph where each community = super-node]
    P5 --> Q3{More than 1 super-node remains?}
    Q3 --> P6[Yes - Repeat Phase 1 on new graph]
    Q3 --> P7[No - Output final community structure]
    P6 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Fraud Ring Detection Workflow Using Girvan-Newman

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Transaction Network Input] --> S2[Construct User-Account Bipartite Graph]
    S2 --> S3[Project to User Similarity Graph]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Edge Betweenness]
    S4 --> S5[Remove Highest Betweenness Edge]
    S5 --> S6[Recompute Betweenness]
    S6 --> S7{Modularity drop significant?}
    S7 --> S8[Yes - Community split detected]
    S7 --> S5
    S8 --> S9[Flag isolated dense subgraphs as fraud rings]
    S9 --> S10[Generate Alert for Investigation]
```

## 4.5 End-to-End Recommendation System Using Community Mining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[User A] --> UN[User-Item Interaction Log]
    U2[User B] --> UN
    U3[User C] --> UN
    UN --> G[Build Bipartite Graph]
    G --> LV[Louvain Community Mining]
    LV --> CM[Community Membership Vector]
    CM --> RS[Collaborative Filtering Recommender]
    RS --> OUT1[Top-N Recommended Items for User A]
    RS --> OUT2[Top-N Recommended Items for User B]
    RS --> OUT3[Top-N Recommended Items for User C]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define community mining in social networks. List any two real-world applications where it is critically deployed.**

**Model Answer:**

Community mining is the process of identifying groups of vertices in a network that are more densely connected internally than with the rest of the network. It leverages the principle of homophily and structural equivalence to discover latent groupings.

**Two real-world applications:**
1. **Fraud Detection in Banking:** Collusive fraud accounts form dense subgraphs with sparse external connections. Community mining algorithms (e.g., Girvan-Newman) expose these fraud rings.
2. **Targeted Marketing in E-Commerce:** Customer purchase networks reveal natural product-affinity communities, enabling personalized recommendation and cross-selling strategies.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 per application with justification)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Explain how the Clique Percolation Method (CPM) differs from the Louvain algorithm in handling overlapping communities.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Clique Percolation Method (CPM)** explicitly allows a node to belong to **multiple communities simultaneously** by percolating $k$-cliques that share $k-1$ nodes. This makes CPM ideal for networks where nodes have multi-faceted roles, such as protein complexes or social actors belonging to several friend groups.

The **Louvain algorithm**, in contrast, produces a **hard partition**—each node is assigned to exactly one community that maximizes modularity gain. While Louvain is faster and scales to billions of edges, it cannot natively represent overlapping memberships.

Hence, CPM is preferred for biological/social networks with inherent overlap, while Louvain dominates in large-scale segmentation tasks where disjoint partitions are acceptable.

*(3 marks: 1 for CPM explanation + 1 for Louvain limitation + 1 for comparative conclusion)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: With a suitable real-world case study, explain how community mining algorithms are applied in detecting fraud rings in financial transaction networks. Discuss the choice of algorithm, the modularity function used, and the challenges faced.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Algorithm Choice & Modularity

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Problem Formalization**
A financial transaction network is modeled as a graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of account holders
- $E$ = set of transactions between accounts (weighted by amount)
- A *fraud ring* is a dense subgraph with high internal transaction volume but sparse external connections.

**Step 2: Choice of Algorithm — Girvan-Newman**
For fraud ring detection on moderately sized forensic graphs ($n \leq 10^5$), the **Girvan-Newman (GN) algorithm** is preferred because:
- Edge betweenness precisely identifies **bridges** that connect otherwise isolated subgraphs.
- Removing high-betweenness edges *cleanly separates* the fraud ring from legitimate users.
- The hierarchical dendrogram produced by GN allows analysts to inspect partitions at multiple granularities.

**Step 3: Modularity Function**
The quality of the resulting partition is evaluated using:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)
$$

A modularity score $Q > 0.3$ typically indicates a statistically significant community structure, flagging the partition as a candidate fraud ring cluster.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Case Study, Challenges & Workflow

**Model Answer:**

**Case Study:** A bank investigates 50,000 accounts suspected of credit-card fraud. The transaction graph contains 180,000 edges (avg degree 7.2). GN is run; the dendrogram reveals **8 distinct communities** with $Q = 0.41$. Six of these communities correspond to legitimate customer clusters (verified by ground-truth labels), while the remaining **2 communities exhibit abnormally high internal transaction density and unusually low external degree**—these are flagged as fraud rings.

**Workflow:**
1. Construct the user-transaction bipartite graph from logs.
2. Project to a user-similarity graph (edges weighted by co-transaction frequency).
3. Apply GN to identify high-betweenness edges.
4. Iteratively remove top-betweenness edges and re-evaluate modularity.
5. Visualize the dendrogram; isolate communities with $Q > 0.3$ and anomalous density ratios.
6. Forward flagged communities to the fraud investigation team.

**Challenges:**
- **Scalability:** GN is $O(m^2 n)$, prohibitively slow for million-edge graphs; workaround is to use approximate variants (e.g., Radatools).
- **Ground truth absence:** Banks rarely have labeled fraud data; modularity becomes the primary quality signal.
- **Evolving fraud patterns:** Static GN cannot handle streaming transactions; streaming Louvain or DLP is preferred for real-time detection.
- **Imbalanced classes:** Legitimate users vastly outnumber fraudsters, requiring density-normalized metrics beyond raw $Q$.

*(Total: 14 marks — 7 for (a) covering formalization + algorithm + formula; 7 for (b) covering case study + workflow + challenges)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: Discuss the application of community mining algorithms in biological networks, specifically in Protein–Protein Interaction (PPI) networks. Explain why the Clique Percolation Method is preferred here and derive the modularity gain for a hypothetical PPI subgraph.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Application in PPI Networks

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: What is a PPI Network?**
A PPI network is a biological graph where:
- Nodes = proteins
- Edges = experimentally verified physical interactions (yeast-two-hybrid, co-IP, etc.)
- A *functional module* (e.g., ribosome, spliceosome, DNA repair complex) corresponds to a **community** in this graph.

**Step 2: Why Community Mining?**
Biologists need to identify which proteins work together to perform a specific cellular function. Communities in PPI networks statistically correlate with known protein complexes, accelerating drug-target discovery and disease-gene prioritization.

**Step 3: Why Clique Percolation Method (CPM)?**
- Biological modules are **inherently overlapping**—a single protein (e.g., p53) participates in multiple complexes (apoptosis, cell-cycle arrest, DNA repair). CPM's $k$-clique adjacency model naturally handles this overlap.
- Dense subgraphs in PPI networks are rich in **$k$-cliques** for $k \geq 3$, making CPM statistically robust.
- Unlike modularity-maximizing methods, CPM does not suffer from the **resolution limit**—it can detect small but biologically critical modules.

**Step 4: Example Application**
In a yeast PPI network with $\sim 6,000$ proteins and $\sim 80,000$ interactions, CPM with $k=3$ successfully identified **85% of known protein complexes** in the MIPS catalog, outperforming modularity-based Louvain (which produced disjoint partitions and missed 22% of multi-complex proteins).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Modularity Gain Derivation

**Model Answer:**

Consider a small PPI subgraph with $m = 12$ edges. A new protein $p$ with degree $k_p = 4$ and $k_{p,in} = 3$ edges into an existing community $C$ with $\Sigma_{in} = 5$ internal edges and $\Sigma_{tot} = 9$ total incident edges.

**The Louvain modularity gain $\Delta Q$ when $p$ joins $C$:**

$$
\Delta Q = \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in} + 2k_{p,in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot} + k_p}{2m} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot}}{2m} \right)^2 - \left( \frac{k_p}{2m} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Substituting values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2m &= 24 \\
\text{New term} &= \frac{5 + 2(3)}{24} - \left( \frac{9 + 4}{24} \right)^2 = \frac{11}{24} - \left( \frac{13}{24} \right)^2 \\
&= 0.4583 - 0.2934 = 0.1649 \\
\text{Old term} &= \frac{5}{24} - \left( \frac{9}{24} \right)^2 - \left( \frac{4}{24} \right)^2 \\
&= 0.2083 - 0.1406 - 0.0278 = 0.0399 \\
\Delta Q &= 0.1649 - 0.0399 = 0.1250
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**
- $2k_{p,in} = 2 \times 3 = 6$ accounts for $p$'s edges that *enter* $C$ and the corresponding $p$-to-outside edges that become internal once $p$ is reassigned.
- The squared terms $\left(\frac{13}{24}\right)^2$ and $\left(\frac{9}{24}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{4}{24}\right)^2$ come from the null-model expected edges under degree-preserving rewiring.
- $\Delta Q = 0.125 > 0$ confirms that moving $p$ into $C$ improves community quality.

**Biological interpretation:** Protein $p$ statistically belongs to the functional module represented by community $C$ — it is likely a previously unannotated member of the complex.

*(Total: 14 marks — 7 for (a) covering biological motivation + CPM rationale; 7 for (b) covering numerical derivation with interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Confusing Modularity Maximization with Graph Partitioning** — they are related but NOT the same. Graph partitioning requires pre-specifying the number of communities; modularity does not. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Omitting Justification for Algorithm Choice** — Simply naming "Louvain" or "Girvan-Newman" is insufficient. KTU examiners expect a *justified* choice based on network size, density, overlap, and dynamicity. State the reason explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping Null Model Explanation in Modularity** — When writing $Q$, students often forget to explain the configuration null model. Examiners specifically look for the term $\frac{k_i k_j}{2m}$ being identified as the **expected** edge probability under random rewiring.
> 4. **Ignoring the Resolution Limit** — Modularity-based methods cannot detect communities smaller than a scale determined by $\sqrt{m}$. Mention this in 14-mark answers for full credit.
> 5. **No Real-World Anchor** — Generic answers lose marks. Always tie the algorithm to a concrete application (fraud, biology, marketing) with a brief case study or numerical example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Community mining identifies densely connected subgraphs; quality is measured by **modularity $Q$** or **conductance $\phi$**.
- **Algorithm Families:** Divisive (Girvan-Newman), Agglomerative (Louvain), Dynamic (Label Propagation), Overlap-aware (CPM), Information-theoretic (Infomap).
- **Application Mapping:**
  - *Social Media & OSNs* → Label Propagation (scale), Louvain (quality)
  - *Biological Networks* → Clique Percolation (overlap)
  - *Fraud Detection* → Girvan-Newman (edge betweenness) or streaming Louvain
  - *Recommendation* → Louvain + Collaborative Filtering
  - *Epidemiology* → Spatio-temporal Louvain
  - *Knowledge Graphs* → Spectral clustering on embeddings
  - *Transportation/IoT* → Edge betweenness for bridge detection
- **Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Modularity: $Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$
  - Edge Betweenness: $C_B(e) = \sum_{s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}$
  - Conductance: $\phi(C) = \frac{\text{cut}(C, \bar{C})}{\min(\text{vol}(C), \text{vol}(\bar{C}))}$
  - Louvain Gain: $\Delta Q$ (greedy local modularity improvement)
  - Jaccard Similarity: $J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$
- **Threshold of Significance:** $Q > 0.3$ indicates strong community structure.
- **Time Complexity Awareness:**
  - Girvan-Newman: $O(m^2 n)$
  - Louvain: $O(m)$ in practice
  - Label Propagation: $O(m)$
  - CPM: exponential in $k$ (use $k=3$ or $k=4$)
- **Resolution Limit Caveat:** Modularity cannot detect communities smaller than $\sim \sqrt{m}$ edges — a critical KTU exam point.
- **Overlap vs. Disjoint:** CPM and SLPA allow overlap; Louvain, GN, spectral methods produce hard partitions.
- **Dynamic Networks:** Static algorithms fail on streaming graphs — use streaming/DLP variants.
- **Always Justify Algorithm Choice** in 14-mark KTU answers; naming alone is insufficient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools for Detecting Communities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools for Detecting Communities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Social Network Analysis (SNA), **community detection** (also called *graph clustering* or *network partitioning*) is the task of identifying groups of vertices (nodes) in a network such that:

- Vertices **within the same group** are densely interconnected (high intra-cluster density).
- Vertices **across different groups** are sparsely connected (low inter-cluster density).

A community is formally defined as a subset of vertices $C \subseteq V$ of a graph $G = (V, E)$ such that the **internal edge density** exceeds the **external edge density** connecting $C$ to the rest of the graph.

The set of algorithms used to perform this partitioning are called **Community Detection Tools** and they constitute a central task in *graph mining*, *network science*, and *computational sociology*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes four principal algorithmic families: (1) **Divisive (Girvan-Newman)**, (2) **Modularity-based (Newman / Louvain)**, (3) **Spectral Clustering**, and (4) **Label Propagation**. A working knowledge of all four is mandatory for ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Network"**
> Imagine 200 people sitting in a large food court. Some groups are chatting loudly (dense internal ties), while different groups barely glance at each other (sparse external ties). A community detection tool is like a *photographer* who wants to take a single group photo capturing only the tightly-knit clusters. The "tools" (Girvan-Newman, Louvain, etc.) are simply different *camera lenses* — each highlights communities differently based on the lens's mathematical focus (e.g., edge bridges, modularity score, or random walks).

## 1.3 Why Communities Matter

- **Marketing:** Target clustered customer segments.
- **Epidemiology:** Identify tightly-knit groups to model disease spread.
- **Biology:** Detect protein complexes in PPI networks.
- **Cybersecurity:** Uncover covert groups in communication graphs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Tools for community detection bridge **unsupervised learning** and **graph theory** — a key inter-disciplinary concept expected in KTU 2024 Scheme.

## 1.4 The Four Core Tools — Quick Overview

| # | Tool | Core Idea | Complexity Class |
|---|------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Girvan-Newman** | Recursively remove edges with highest **edge betweenness** | $O(m^2 n)$ |
| 2 | **Newman Modularity Maximization** | Greedily merge communities to maximize $Q$ | $O((n+m)n)$ |
| 3 | **Louvain Method** | Iterative two-phase modularity optimization | $O(n \log n)$ |
| 4 | **Spectral Clustering** | Use eigenvectors of graph **Laplacian** | $O(n^3)$ |
| 5 | **Label Propagation (LPA)** | Spread labels from random seeds | $O(n+m)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tool 1 — Girvan-Newman Algorithm (Divisive Method)

**Core Principle:** Communities are joined by a relatively small number of *inter-community edges*. If we remove these "bridge" edges, the network falls apart into natural communities.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. Compute **edge betweenness centrality** for *every* edge in the network.
2. Remove the edge(s) with the **highest betweenness**.
3. Recalculate edge betweenness on the modified graph.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until no edges remain (or a target number of communities is reached).
5. The **dendrogram** produced yields the hierarchical community structure.

### Definition: Edge Betweenness

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge Betweenness Centrality** of an edge $e$ is defined as the number of **shortest paths** between all pairs of vertices $(i, j)$ that pass through $e$.

$$C_B(e) = \sum_{s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{st}$ = total number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$.
- $\sigma_{st}(e)$ = number of those shortest paths that traverse edge $e$.

## 2.2 Tool 2 — Modularity Maximization (Newman-Girvan, 2004)

**Core Principle:** A good community structure has *more* internal edges than expected by chance. Modularity $Q$ quantifies this.

$$Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \cdot \delta(c_i, c_j)$$

where:
- $A_{ij}$ = adjacency matrix entry.
- $k_i, k_j$ = degrees of nodes $i$ and $j$.
- $m = \vert E \vert$ = total number of edges.
- $c_i, c_j$ = community labels.
- $\delta(\cdot,\cdot)$ = **Kronecker delta** (= 1 if same community, 0 otherwise).
- $2m = \sum_i k_i$.

**Interpretation:** $Q$ compares the actual fraction of intra-community edges to the expected fraction in a *null model* (configuration model with same degree sequence). $Q \in [-0.5, 1]$. **Higher $Q$ = better community structure.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **modularity** (scalar score, $Q$) with **modularity matrix** $B$ (an $n \times n$ matrix).

### Modularity Matrix

$$B_{ij} = A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}$$

Summing $B_{ij}$ over a community $c$ gives $2m \cdot Q_c$.

## 2.3 Tool 3 — Louvain Method (Blondel et al., 2008)

A two-phase greedy algorithm:

1. **Phase 1 (Local Moving):** Each node joins the community of one of its neighbors if it yields the **maximum positive modularity gain** $\Delta Q$.
2. **Phase 2 (Aggregation):** Build a new graph where each community is a super-node, with edge weights = sum of inter-community edges.
3. Repeat phases 1 & 2 until no further modularity gain is possible.

### Modularity Gain Formula

$$\Delta Q = \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in} + 2k_{i,in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot} + k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right] - \left[ \frac{\Sigma_{in}}{2m} - \left( \frac{\Sigma_{tot}}{2m} \right)^2 - \left( \frac{k_i}{2m} \right)^2 \right]$$

where:
- $\Sigma_{in}$ = sum of weights of edges inside community $C$.
- $\Sigma_{tot}$ = sum of weights of all edges incident to nodes in $C$.
- $k_i$ = degree of node $i$.
- $k_{i,in}$ = sum of weights of edges from $i$ to nodes in $C$.

## 2.4 Tool 4 — Spectral Clustering

**Core Principle:** Use eigenvectors of the **graph Laplacian** to embed the graph in a low-dimensional space, then apply classical clustering (e.g., $k$-means).

### Graph Laplacian

$$L = D - A$$

where $D$ = diagonal degree matrix, $A$ = adjacency matrix.

### Normalized Laplacian (Symmetric Form)

$$L_{sym} = D^{-1/2} L D^{-1/2} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$$

### Algorithm Steps

1. Construct the Laplacian $L$.
2. Compute the **first $k$ smallest non-trivial eigenvectors** of $L$.
3. Stack these eigenvectors as columns to form matrix $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.
4. Normalize each row of $U$ to unit length.
5. Apply $k$-means on the row vectors to obtain $k$ clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Laplacian?** It is **positive semi-definite** with $n$ non-negative eigenvalues $0 = \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq \dots \leq \lambda_n$. The number of **zero eigenvalues** equals the number of **connected components** — a deep theoretical connection that KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.5 Tool 5 — Label Propagation Algorithm (LPA)

**Core Principle:** Nodes adopt the label most frequent among their neighbors. Random initial labels converge to a community partition.

### Steps

1. Initialize: every node gets a unique label.
2. Iterate: for each node (in random order), assign the label that is **most frequent** among its neighbors (ties broken randomly).
3. Stop when no node changes label.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Edge Betweenness | $C_B(e) = \sum_{s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | Girvan-Newman |
| Modularity | $Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$ | Range $[-0.5, 1]$ |
| Modularity Matrix | $B_{ij} = A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}$ | Used in spectral modularity |
| Graph Laplacian | $L = D - A$ | PSD matrix |
| Normalized Laplacian | $L_{sym} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$ | Used in Shi-Malik algorithm |
| Number of zero eigenvalues of $L$ | $\lambda_0 = 0$, multiplicity = $\#\text{components}$ | Key theorem |
| Conductance of cut $(S, \bar S)$ | $\phi(S) = \frac{\vert E(S, \bar S) \vert}{\min(\text{vol}(S), \text{vol}(\bar S))}$ | Spectral guarantee |
| Clustering Coefficient (local) | $C_i = \frac{2 \vert e_{jk} : v_j, v_k \in N_i \vert}{k_i(k_i - 1)}$ | Density metric |
| Walktrap distance | $r_{ij} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{(P_{ik}^t - P_{jk}^t)^2}{d(k)}}$ | Pons-Latapy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Spectral clustering is widely used in **image segmentation** (computer vision), **load balancing** (parallel computing), and **VLSI design** (circuit partitioning). Modularity optimization dominates **recommender systems** and **biological network analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Modularity as a "Null Model" Comparison

### Starting Point

The fraction of edges that fall **inside** communities (sum over communities $c$):

$$
\frac{\text{edges inside communities}}{m} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{c} \sum_{i,j \in c} A_{ij}
$$

### Expected Fraction Under Null Model

In the **configuration model**, edges are placed randomly while preserving node degrees. The probability of an edge between $i$ and $j$ is:

$$
P_{ij} = \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}
$$

Since $A_{ij}$ is the *actual* adjacency, the expected value $\mathbb{E}[A_{ij}] = k_i k_j / 2m$.

### Building the Difference

Subtracting expected from actual:

$$
\left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right]
$$

Now summing over all pairs $(i, j)$ **only when they are in the same community**:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \cdot \delta(c_i, c_j)
$$

> **Logic in plain English:** We measure the *surprise*: how many more (or fewer) intra-community edges exist compared to random chance. A high $Q$ means the network is *not random* — it has a clear community structure.

### Modularity of a Single Community

For a community $c$ with internal edges $L_c$ and sum of degrees $K_c$:

$$
Q_c = \frac{L_c}{m} - \left( \frac{K_c}{2m} \right)^2
$$

Total $Q$ is the sum: $Q = \sum_c Q_c$.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: 4-Node Graph

Consider the *toy graph* with 4 nodes forming a square: $A-B-C-D-A$ plus a *diagonal* $A-C$ (5 edges).

| Edge | Endpoint 1 | Endpoint 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $e_1$ | A | B |
| $e_2$ | B | C |
| $e_3$ | C | D |
| $e_4$ | D | A |
| $e_5$ | A | C |

### Step 1: Compute degrees

$k_A = 3, k_B = 2, k_C = 3, k_D = 2$, so $2m = 10$.

### Step 2: Test community split {A, C} vs {B, D}

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j \in c} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{10} \left[ \left(1 - \frac{3 \cdot 3}{10}\right) + \left(1 - \frac{2 \cdot 2}{10}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{10} \left[ \left(1 - 0.9\right) + \left(1 - 0.4\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{10} \left[ 0.1 + 0.6 \right] = \frac{0.7}{10} = 0.07
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $Q = 0.07 > 0$, so the partition {A, C} / {B, D} is a valid community structure (weakly).

> [!NOTE]
> This exact problem appears in KTU model papers. The student must explicitly compute $2m$, the adjacency entries, and the degree products.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Girvan-Newman on a Triangle-Star Graph

Consider a graph: Triangle $\{1, 2, 3\}$ connected via edge $(3, 4)$ to a Triangle $\{4, 5, 6\}$.

### Step 1: Compute shortest paths between all pairs

- Pairs within Triangle 1: 3 pairs, 1 shortest path each, no edge between 1&3.
- Pairs within Triangle 2: 3 pairs, 1 shortest path each, no edge between 5&6.
- **Cross pairs** (one from each triangle): $3 \times 3 = 9$ pairs.
- For cross pairs, all 9 shortest paths go through the *bridge edge* $(3, 4)$.

### Step 2: Edge Betweenness

- **Edge $(3, 4)$:** $\sigma_{st}$ that use it = 9. So $C_B = 9$.
- **Edge $(1, 2)$:** Used by paths like $3-1-2$? No, direct paths exist. So $C_B = 0$ for most triangle edges.

> **Result:** The bridge $(3, 4)$ is removed first, splitting the graph into two clean triangles — exactly the two ground-truth communities.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Spectral Clustering (3-Cycle Example)

Let $G$ be a triangle: $A_{ij} = 1$ if $i \neq j$, $0$ otherwise.

### Step 1: Adjacency and Degree

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: Laplacian

$$
L = D - A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Eigenvalues

Characteristic polynomial: $\det(L - \lambda I) = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det \begin{pmatrix} 2-\lambda & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2-\lambda & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2-\lambda \end{pmatrix} &= 0 \\
(2-\lambda)^3 - 3(2-\lambda) - 2 &= 0 \\
\lambda^3 - 6\lambda^2 + 9\lambda &= 0 \\
\lambda(\lambda - 3)^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 = 3, \lambda_3 = 3$.

### Step 4: Interpretation

- Multiplicity of $\lambda = 0$ is **1** → the graph has **1 connected component** (the whole triangle).
- Spectral gap: $\lambda_2 - \lambda_1 = 3$ → the graph is "clusterable" into $k = 1$ community.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | Girvan-Newman | Newman Modularity | Louvain | Spectral | LPA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Complexity** | $O(m^2 n)$ | $O((n+m)n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n+m)$ |
| **Resolution Limit** | No | Yes (Newman) | Yes | No | No |
| **Deterministic?** | Yes | Yes | No (ties) | Yes | No (random) |
| **Hierarchy?** | Yes (dendrogram) | No (flat) | Yes | No | No |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Medium | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| **Quality (Benchmark)** | High | High | Very High | Medium | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution Limit Theorem (Fortunato-Barthélemy, 2007):** Modularity optimization *cannot* detect communities smaller than a scale $\sqrt{m}$. This is a **favorite KTU question** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture of Community Detection Tools

The following block diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the five principal tools and the data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G equals V comma E] --> B[Tool Family Selector]
    B --> C1[Divisive Family]
    B --> C2[Modularity Family]
    B --> C3[Spectral Family]
    B --> C4[Propagation Family]
    C1 --> D1[Girvan Newman Algorithm]
    C2 --> D2[Newman Greedy Merge]
    C2 --> D3[Louvain Two Phase]
    C3 --> D4[Build Laplacian L equals D minus A]
    D4 --> D5[Compute First k Eigenvectors]
    D5 --> D6[Apply k Means Clustering]
    C4 --> D7[Label Propagation LPA]
    D1 --> E[Output Communities or Dendrogram]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    E --> F[Evaluate Q Modularity Score]
    E --> G[Evaluate NMI and ARI Metrics]
    F --> H[Return Optimal Partition]
    G --> H
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Flow: Girvan-Newman Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start with Graph G] --> compute1[Compute Edge Betweenness for all edges]
    compute1 --> findmax[Find edge e with maximum betweenness]
    findmax --> remove[Remove edge e from G]
    remove --> check{Is target number of communities reached or no edges remain?}
    check -->|No| compute1
    check -->|Yes| build[Build Dendrogram from removal order]
    build --> cut[Cut dendrogram at maximum Q score]
    cut --> output[Output Final Communities]
```

## 4.3 Louvain Method — Two-Phase Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1Start[Phase 1: Local Moving] --> p1A[Initialize each node as its own community]
    p1A --> p1B[For each node i in random order]
    p1B --> p1C[Compute delta Q for moving i to each neighbor's community]
    p1C --> p1D{Max delta Q greater than 0?}
    p1D -->|Yes| p1E[Move i to best neighbor community]
    p1D -->|No| p1F[Keep i in current community]
    p1E --> p1G{Any node moved?}
    p1F --> p1G
    p1G -->|Yes| p1B
    p1G -->|No| p2Start[Phase 2: Graph Aggregation]
    p2Start --> p2A[Build new graph where each community is a supernode]
    p2A --> p2B[Edge weight equals sum of inter community edges]
    p2B --> p2C{Modularity increased?}
    p2C -->|Yes| p1Start
    p2C -->|No| done[Output Final Communities]
```

## 4.4 Spectral Clustering — Eigen-Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sStart[Input Adjacency Matrix A] --> sBuild[Build Laplacian L equals D minus A]
    sBuild --> sNorm[Optional: Normalize L sym equals D to power minus 0.5 times L times D to power minus 0.5]
    sNorm --> sEig[Compute first k smallest eigenvectors]
    sEig --> sMatrix[Form matrix U in R n by k]
    sMatrix --> sRow[Normalize each row of U to unit length]
    sRow --> sKmeans[Apply k means on row vectors]
    sKmeans --> sOut[Output k Clusters]
```

## 4.5 Decision Logic — Which Tool to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart{Graph size n and m} -->|n less than 1000| qBranch1{Time budget}
    qStart -->|n greater than 10000| qBranch2{Quality required}
    qBranch1 -->|High| qA1[Use Girvan Newman]
    qBranch1 -->|Medium| qA2[Use Spectral]
    qBranch1 -->|Low| qA3[Use Newman Modularity]
    qBranch2 -->|High| qB1[Use Louvain]
    qBranch2 -->|Medium| qB2[Use Label Propagation]
    qBranch2 -->|Low| qB3[Use Any Scalable Tool]
```
