### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**What is the privacy budget $\varepsilon$ in differential privacy? Comment on the trade-off involved.**

**Model Answer:**

The **privacy budget $\varepsilon$** is a non-negative real number that bounds the multiplicative change in output probability when one record is added or removed from the dataset:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

**Trade-off:**
- **Smaller $\varepsilon$** → stronger privacy → more noise added → lower data utility.
- **Larger $\varepsilon$** → weaker privacy → less noise → higher utility.

Typical industry choices: $\varepsilon \in [0.1, 3]$. Apple uses $\varepsilon \approx 1$–$8$ in iOS; the US Census 2020 used $\varepsilon = 17$ (with hierarchical composition).

**(End — 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Narayanan-Shmatikov de-anonymization attack** on social networks. How does it exploit auxiliary graph information to re-identify anonymized users?

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a suitable example dataset, demonstrate the process of achieving **3-anonymity** through **generalization** and **suppression** of quasi-identifiers. Verify whether the resulting dataset satisfies 2-diversity.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Background [1 mark]:**
Narayanan and Shmatikov (2009) demonstrated that anonymized social graphs are *not* safe. They re-identified users in the **Netflix Prize dataset** (anonymized movie ratings) using the **IMDB dataset** (public, with usernames) as auxiliary information.

**2. Threat Model [1 mark]:**
- **Auxiliary graph $G_{aux}$** = small set of users whose identity is publicly known + their relationships.
- **Target graph $G_{anon}$** = large published anonymized graph.
- The adversary knows a few **seed nodes** that exist in both graphs.

**3. Algorithm Steps [3 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Seed Identification:**
The adversary locates a small set of "anchor" users in $G_{anon}$ that likely correspond to the same users in $G_{aux}$. Common heuristics:
- Match users with **rare attribute combinations**.
- Match users with **distinctive degree sequences**.

**Step 2 — Propagation (Breadth-First Matching):**
For each matched pair $(u_i, v_i)$, expand the match to neighbors using structural similarity:

$$\text{sim}(v, w) = \frac{\vert N(v) \cap M(N(u)) \vert}{\vert N(v) \cup M(N(u)) \vert}$$

where $M$ is the current partial mapping. This is a **Jaccard-like ego-net similarity**.

**Step 3 — Confidence Scoring:**
Each new candidate match receives a confidence score. Low-confidence matches are rejected to control the **false-positive rate**.

**Step 4 — Output:**
A high-confidence mapping of $G_{aux}$ users to $G_{anon}$ users.

**4. Results [1 mark]:**
On the Netflix dataset, with knowledge of just **8 movie ratings** of a user (and approximate dates), the adversary re-identified **> 99%** of users.

**5. Countermeasures [1 mark]:**
- **Edge randomization** (e.g., random deletion/addition)
- **Differential privacy** in graph release
- **k-degree anonymity** — ensure every degree appears $\geq k$ times.

**(End of 7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data (un-anonymized microdata):**

| ID | Age | ZIP | Gender | Disease |
|----|-----|-----|--------|---------|
| 1  | 25  | 56789 | M | Flu |
| 2  | 25  | 56789 | M | Diabetes |
| 3  | 30  | 12345 | F | Cancer |
| 4  | 30  | 12345 | F | Flu |
| 5  | 40  | 67890 | M | HIV |
| 6  | 40  | 67890 | M | Flu |

**Quasi-Identifier (QI) = {Age, ZIP, Gender}**, **Sensitive Attribute (SA) = Disease**.

**Step 1 — Generalize Age and ZIP to achieve 3-anonymity [3 marks]:**

Apply **Generalization Hierarchy**:
- Age → ranges: $[20\text{–}29]$, $[30\text{–}39]$, $[40\text{–}49]$
- ZIP → prefixes: $567**$, $123**$, $678**$

| Generalized Age | Generalized ZIP | Gender | Disease | Count |
|------------------|-----------------|--------|---------|-------|
| [20–29]          | 567**           | M      | Flu, Diabetes | 2 |
| [30–39]          | 123**           | F      | Cancer, Flu   | 2 |
| [40–49]          | 678**           | M      | HIV, Flu      | 2 |

Each group has only **2** records → **3-anonymity NOT satisfied**.

**Apply Suppression to one record + further generalization [1 mark]:**

We **suppress** the ZIP of record 5 and generalize Age to broader ranges:

| Generalized Age | Generalized ZIP | Gender | Disease | Count |
|------------------|-----------------|--------|---------|-------|
| [*]              | 5****           | *      | Flu, Diabetes | 2 |
| [*]              | 1****           | *      | Cancer, Flu   | 2 |
| [*]              | 6****           | *      | HIV, Flu      | 2 |

Still only 2 per group. **Merge all into one large group via total generalization [1 mark]:**

| Generalized Age | Generalized ZIP | Gender | Disease | Count |
|------------------|-----------------|--------|---------|-------|
| [*]              | ******          | *      | Flu, Diabetes, Cancer, Flu, HIV, Flu | **6** |

Now each equivalence class (the single class) has 6 ≥ 3 → **3-anonymity SATISFIED** ✓

**Step 2 — Check 2-Diversity [2 marks]:**
The single equivalence class contains diseases: {Flu (×3), Diabetes, Cancer, HIV} → **4 distinct sensitive values**.

Since $4 \geq 2$, the dataset is **2-diverse (and in fact 4-diverse)** ✓

**Step 3 — Trade-off Discussion [Bonus / 1 mark]:**
While privacy is preserved, the **utility is severely reduced** — almost all attribute specificity is lost. This illustrates the **privacy–utility trade-off**, a key concept in differential privacy.

**(End of 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative for Internal Choice) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Differential Privacy**. Prove that the **Laplace Mechanism** satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy. State the meaning of $\varepsilon$, sensitivity $\Delta f$, and noise scale $b$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A hospital wants to release the **average age** of patients in two wards. Ward A has 1000 patients, Ward B has 1500 patients. Using the Laplace Mechanism with $\varepsilon = 1$ and assuming sensitivity of "average" query is $\Delta f = 1/1000$ (due to age range normalization), compute the noise to be added. Discuss how sequential composition affects the overall privacy budget if both ward averages are released.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition [2 marks]:**

A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ is $\varepsilon$-differentially private if for all neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ (differing in one record) and all output sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

**Intuition:** Including or excluding any single user changes the output probability by at most a factor of $e^{\varepsilon}$. The adversary learns essentially nothing about an individual's contribution.

**Proof that Laplace Mechanism satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP [4 marks]:**

(See the full derivation in SECTION 3.1. Summary:)

Step 1: PDF of $\text{Lap}(b)$ is $p(x) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp(-\vert x \vert / b)$.

Step 2: For output $z$, $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = z] = p(z - f(D))$.

Step 3: Ratio of probabilities:
$$\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = z]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = z]} = \exp\left(\frac{\Vert z - f(D') \Vert_1 - \Vert z - f(D) \Vert_1}{b}\right)$$

Step 4: By the **reverse triangle inequality** and the **definition of sensitivity**:
$$\Vert z - f(D') \Vert_1 - \Vert z - f(D) \Vert_1 \leq \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1 \leq \Delta f$$

Step 5: Substituting $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$:
$$\text{Ratio} \leq \exp(\Delta f / b) = \exp(\varepsilon)$$

Step 6: By symmetry, the lower bound is $\exp(-\varepsilon)$. Hence the mechanism is $\varepsilon$-DP. **Q.E.D.**

**Meaning of terms [1 mark]:**
- $\varepsilon$ (epsilon): **Privacy budget** — smaller means stronger privacy.
- $\Delta f$: **Global sensitivity** — max change in query output between neighboring datasets.
- $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$: **Noise scale** — controls the magnitude of Laplace noise added.

**(End of 7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Ward A: $n_A = 1000$, Ward B: $n_B = 1500$.
- Privacy budget per query: $\varepsilon = 1$.
- Sensitivity of average query: $\Delta f = 1/1000 = 0.001$.

**Step 1 — Noise Scale for Each Query [2 marks]:**

For Ward A:
$$b_A = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.001}{1} = 0.001$$

For Ward B:
$$b_B = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.001}{1} = 0.001$$

**Step 2 — Expected Magnitude of Noise [2 marks]:**

The expected absolute noise from a Laplace distribution with scale $b$ is $E[|X|] = b$.

So expected noise for each query = $0.001$ years. If true averages are, say, 45 and 52, the **published** values are approximately $45.001$ and $52.001$ — a **negligible perturbation** but with formal privacy guarantee.

**Step 3 — Sequential Composition [2 marks]:**

When the **same dataset is used** for multiple queries (or queries on overlapping wards), privacy budgets **add up**:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \varepsilon_i = 1 + 1 = 2$$

So releasing both ward averages with $\varepsilon = 1$ each results in an **overall $\varepsilon = 2$** guarantee — **weaker privacy** than the per-query guarantee.

**Step 4 — Mitigation [1 mark]:**
To maintain total $\varepsilon = 1$, allocate budget as $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 0.5$, doubling the noise to $b = 0.002$.

**(End of 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the role of $\Delta f$** — Many students write "add noise" without computing sensitivity. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing sequential and parallel composition** — Always check: is the dataset *the same* (sequential, additive) or *disjoint* (parallel, max)?
> 3. **Skipping the formal definition of $k$-anonymity** — KTU examiners expect the *quantitative* statement $\vert \cdot \vert \geq k$, not just a verbal description. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting to discuss the privacy-utility trade-off** — Almost every privacy model has this trade-off; mentioning it earns a bonus 1 mark.
> 5. **Not specifying the noise distribution** in Laplace Mechanism answers — Always state $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Privacy in OSNs**

- [x] **Privacy in OSNs** is the multi-dimensional protection of *identity, links, attributes, and content* of users.
- [x] An **adversary** can be *passive, active, global, or local* — always specify the threat model.
- [x] **k-Anonymity** guarantees that every record is in a QI-group of size $\geq k$.
- [x] **k-Anonymity FAILS** against **homogeneity attack** and **background knowledge attack**.
- [x] **l-Diversity** requires $\geq l$ "well-represented" sensitive values per equivalence class.
- [x] **t-Closeness** requires the sensitive attribute distribution in each class to be within distance $t$ (EMD) of the global distribution.
- [x] **Differential Privacy (DP)** is the *mathematically strongest* privacy model.
- [x] **$\varepsilon$-DP definition:** $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$.
- [x] **Laplace Mechanism:** Add $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ noise to numeric queries.
- [x] **Sequential Composition:** $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$ (same dataset).
- [x] **Parallel Composition:** $\varepsilon_{total} = \max \varepsilon_i$ (disjoint datasets).
- [x] **Narayanan-Shmatikov (2009)** is the landmark de-anonymization attack — know its 4-step pipeline.
- [x] **Structural attacks** use *degree sequence, ego-networks, Jaccard, Cosine, Adamic-Adar*.
- [x] **Real-world deployment:** Apple, Google, US Census 2020, Microsoft all use DP.
- [x] **Always discuss privacy–utility trade-off** in 14-mark answers.
- [x] **GDPR Article 25, India's DPDP Act 2023, and CCPA** mandate privacy-by-design — relevant for application-based questions.
- [x] **Common pitfalls:** confusing $k$ and $l$; miscomputing $\Delta f$; forgetting composition theorem.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Privacy in Online Social Networks (PECDT863, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Trust in Online Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trust in Online Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Trust in an Online Environment

In the context of **Social Network Analysis (SNA)** and the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Trust in an Online Environment** is formally defined as a measurable, directional, and subjective belief held by a *trustor* (source node) about the *trustee* (target node) regarding the reliability, integrity, competence, and predictability of the trustee's future actions, opinions, or content within a digital, computer-mediated social network.

Mathematically, it can be represented as a weighted, directed edge in a social trust graph:

$$T: V \times V \rightarrow [-1, +1]$$

where $T(u, v)$ is the **trust score** assigned by user $u$ (trustor) to user $v$ (trustee). Positive values denote trust, negative values denote distrust, and zero denotes neutrality or absence of opinion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Trust is NOT just a binary (trust/distrust) variable. It is a **continuous, asymmetric, and transitive** property. Asymmetry means $T(u,v) \neq T(v,u)$, and transitivity allows trust to "flow" through intermediaries — a property central to recommender systems and reputation engines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are moving to a new city and you need a good dentist. You do not know any dentist personally. In the **offline world**, you might:
- Ask a close friend (whom you trust) for a recommendation.
- That friend, if she doesn't know one, asks her trusted colleague.
- Eventually, you get a referral from someone you have never met, but the chain of trust makes the recommendation credible.

**Online trust works identically** — except the chain is encoded as graph edges instead of physical handshakes. A user $A$ follows user $B$, who follows user $C$, who posts a glowing review of vendor $D$. The platform's trust engine "walks" this graph and assigns a propagated trust score to $D$ from $A$'s perspective.

Think of trust as **"social gravity"** — the closer (fewer hops) and stronger the edges to a node, the heavier (more credible) its influence is on you.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary You MUST Know for the KTU Exam:**

| Term | Definition | Notation |
|------|------------|----------|
| **Trustor** | The agent who places trust | Source node $u$ |
| **Trustee** | The agent who is trusted | Target node $v$ |
| **Trust Score** | Quantified level of confidence | $T(u,v) \in [-1, 1]$ |
| **Trust Propagation** | Inference of trust through intermediaries | Path-based computation |
| **Direct Trust** | Trust based on first-hand experience | $T_{direct}$ |
| **Recommended/Indirect Trust** | Trust inferred via a third party | $T_{indirect}$ |
| **Reputation** | Aggregated global opinion about a node | $R(v)$ |
| **Trust Network** | Directed graph where edges represent trust | $G = (V, E, W)$ |
| **Trust Threshold** | Minimum score required to act on a recommendation | $\tau$ |

## 1.4 Why is Online Trust a Hard Problem?

The **FOAM framework** (commonly cited in literature) summarizes the four properties of online trust:

1. **Fuzzy** — Trust is not crisp; it has degrees.
2. **Opinion-based** — Formed from subjective evidence (reviews, ratings).
3. **Asymmetric** — $A$ trusts $B$ does not imply $B$ trusts $A$.
4. **Multidimensional** — Built on ability, benevolence, and integrity.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** "**FAIM**" — Fuzzy, Asymmetric, Inferred (transitive), Multidimensional.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Online Trust

According to the Mayer-Davis-Schoorman trust model (adapted to online environments), online trust rests on three core pillars:

### Pillar 1: **Trust Antecedents** (What creates trust?)
- **Ability** — Perceived competence of the trustee.
- **Benevolence** — Belief that the trustee acts in the trustor's interest.
- **Integrity** — Perception that the trustee adheres to acceptable principles.

### Pillar 2: **Trust Building Blocks** (How is it formed?)
- **Direct Experience** — Past transactions/interactions.
- **Reputation Systems** — Aggregated peer ratings (e.g., eBay seller scores).
- **Third-party Certification** — Trust seals, verified badges.
- **Social Proof** — Network-based endorsements (likes, follows).

### Pillar 3: **Trust Outcomes** (What does trust enable?)
- Willingness to transact, share information, accept recommendations, and form network ties.

## 2.2 Types of Trust in Online Environments

| Type | Description | Example Platform |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Interpersonal Trust** | Trust between two individuals | Facebook friends |
| **System Trust** | Trust in the platform/infrastructure | Banking apps |
| **Dispositional Trust** | Personality-based tendency to trust | Trusting vs. cynical users |
| **Cognitive Trust** | Based on evidence, reasoning, reputation | Verified seller ratings |
| **Affective Trust** | Based on emotional bonds, familiarity | Long-time online friends |
| **Institutional Trust** | Trust in the governing body/rules | Wikipedia policies |

## 2.3 Trust Computation Models (The Heart of the Topic)

### Model 1: Simple Aggregation
The most basic model — average all ratings given to a trustee:

$$R(v) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i(v)$$

where $r_i(v)$ is the rating given to $v$ by user $i$, and $n$ is the number of raters. **Limitation:** Treats all raters equally — a known liar weighs the same as a verified expert.

### Model 2: Weighted Trust Aggregation
Each rater $i$ is weighted by the trust the trustor $u$ places in $i$:

$$R(v) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T(u, i) \cdot r_i(v)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T(u, i)}$$

This is the **foundational equation** for most modern recommender systems.

### Model 3: Trust Propagation (Transitive Trust)
If $u$ trusts $i$ and $i$ trusts $v$, then trust propagates:

$$T(u, v) = T(u, i) \otimes T(i, v)$$

where $\otimes$ is a **trust composition operator**. Common operators:

| Operator | Formula | Use Case |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **Multiplicative** | $T(u,v) = T(u,i) \cdot T(i,v)$ | Most common, conservative |
| **Minimum (T-conorm)** | $T(u,v) = \min(T(u,i), T(i,v))$ | Pessimistic, weak-link |
| **Weighted average** | $T(u,v) = w_1 T_1 + w_2 T_2$ | Multi-path aggregation |

### Model 4: EigenTrust (Global Reputation)
Proposed by Kamvar, Schlosser, and Garcia-Molina (2003), EigenTrust computes a **global trust value** for every node by finding the **left eigenvector** of the trust matrix. It is essentially a PageRank variant for trust networks.

Let $\mathbf{C}$ be the normalized trust matrix. The EigenTrust vector $\vec{t}$ satisfies:

$$\vec{t} = \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}$$

Subject to $\sum_{j} t_j = 1$. In practice, it is iteratively computed as:

$$\vec{t}^{(k+1)} = (1 - d) \cdot \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(k)} + d \cdot \vec{p}$$

where $d$ is a damping factor (typically $d = 0.15$) and $\vec{p}$ is a pre-trusted distribution that prevents malicious nodes from gaming the system.

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU ESE questions:**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | $R(v) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i} r_i(v)$ | Simple reputation | Basic aggregation |
| 2 | $R(v) = \frac{\sum_i T(u,i) \cdot r_i(v)}{\sum_i T(u,i)}$ | Weighted reputation | Personalized trust |
| 3 | $T(u,v) = T(u,i) \cdot T(i,v)$ | Transitive trust (mult.) | Path-based scoring |
| 4 | $T(u,v) = \min(T(u,i), T(i,v))$ | Weakest-link trust | Conservative systems |
| 5 | $\vec{t} = (1-d)\mathbf{C}^T\vec{t} + d\vec{p}$ | EigenTrust iteration | Global reputation |
| 6 | $\tau = \text{threshold}$ for action | Trust threshold | Decision-making |
| 7 | $\text{Trust}_k = \alpha \cdot C + \beta \cdot R + \gamma \cdot P$ | Composite trust | Ability+Rep+Prov. |

In Formula 7: $C$ = Competence, $R$ = Reputation, $P$ = Provenance, and $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, eBay) — seller reputation systems.
- **Peer-to-peer (P2P) networks** — EigenTrust for file-sharing trust.
- **Online review systems** (Yelp, TripAdvisor) — weighted reviewer scores.
- **Blockchain/Web3** — decentralized trust via consensus.
- **Recommender systems** — trust-aware collaborative filtering.
- **Cybersecurity** — trust-based access control (TrustBAC, TMACS).
- **Healthcare social networks** — trust in patient communities (PatientsLikeMe).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Trust Propagation Over a Multi-Hop Path

**Problem Setup:** Consider a trust network where user $A$ trusts user $B$ with score $0.8$, $B$ trusts $C$ with score $0.7$, and $C$ trusts $D$ with score $0.6$. Compute the propagated trust $T(A, D)$ using:
- (i) Multiplicative composition
- (ii) Minimum (weakest-link) composition
- (iii) Weighted path aggregation assuming there is also a second path $A \rightarrow E \rightarrow D$ with edge weights $0.5$ and $0.9$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the graph and edges.**

$$\text{Path 1: } A \xrightarrow{0.8} B \xrightarrow{0.7} C \xrightarrow{0.6} D$$

$$\text{Path 2: } A \xrightarrow{0.5} E \xrightarrow{0.9} D$$

**Step 2 — Apply Multiplicative Composition along Path 1.**

For multiplicative trust composition, the rule is:

$$T(u, v) = \prod_{e \in \text{path}(u,v)} w(e)$$

We substitute the edge weights:

$$T_1(A, D) = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.6$$

Perform the multiplication stepwise:

$$T_1(A, D) = 0.56 \times 0.6 = 0.336$$

> **Logic:** Trust decays multiplicatively along a chain. Each intermediate node "filters" the signal — just like a resistor network in electrical engineering, where series resistors multiply their resistances.

**Step 3 — Apply Multiplicative Composition along Path 2.**

$$T_2(A, D) = 0.5 \times 0.9 = 0.45$$

> **Logic:** This path, although shorter (only 2 hops), is stronger than Path 1 because the intermediate trust is higher.

**Step 4 — Apply Minimum (Weakest-Link) Composition along Path 1.**

For the weakest-link operator:

$$T_{\min}(A, D) = \min(0.8, 0.7, 0.6) = 0.6$$

> **Logic:** The "bottleneck" of the chain — the weakest trust link — governs. This is the conservative model: a chain is only as strong as its weakest member.

**Step 5 — Aggregate Multiple Paths (Weighted Average).**

When multiple independent paths exist, trust scores are combined. The simplest multi-path aggregation is the **arithmetic mean** of path trusts:

$$T_{\text{agg}}(A, D) = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{n_{\text{paths}}}$$

Substitute the values:

$$T_{\text{agg}}(A, D) = \frac{0.336 + 0.45}{2}$$

Perform the addition:

$$T_{\text{agg}}(A, D) = \frac{0.786}{2} = 0.393$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Trust Threshold.**

Suppose the platform requires a minimum trust of $\tau = 0.4$ before recommending $D$ to $A$.

- Aggregated score: $0.393 < 0.4$ → **System REJECTS** the recommendation.
- Path 2 alone: $0.45 \geq 0.4$ → **If only Path 2 existed, the system would ACCEPT.**

> **Insight:** The aggregation can dilute a strong path through averaging. Many systems use **max-of-paths** instead: $T = \max(T_1, T_2) = 0.45$ to avoid this dilution.

## 3.2 Derivation: EigenTrust Iterative Update (Toy Example)

**Setup:** A small P2P network with 3 nodes. The trust matrix $\mathbf{C}$ (column-normalized) is given as:

$$\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.6 & 0.0 & 0.4 \\ 0.4 & 0.5 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Damping factor $d = 0.15$. Pre-trusted vector $\vec{p} = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]^T$. Initial trust vector $\vec{t}^{(0)} = \vec{p}$.

**Iteration Rule (from Section 2.3, Model 4):**

$$\vec{t}^{(k+1)} = (1 - d) \cdot \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(k)} + d \cdot \vec{p}$$

### Iteration 1

Compute $\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(0)}$:

$$\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0 & 0.6 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.0 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $0.0(0.5) + 0.6(0.3) + 0.4(0.2) = 0.18 + 0.08 = 0.26$
Row 2: $0.5(0.5) + 0.0(0.3) + 0.5(0.2) = 0.25 + 0.10 = 0.35$
Row 3: $0.3(0.5) + 0.4(0.3) + 0.3(0.2) = 0.15 + 0.12 + 0.06 = 0.33$

So $\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(0)} = [0.26, 0.35, 0.33]^T$.

Apply the full update with $1 - d = 0.85$:

$$t_1^{(1)} = 0.85(0.26) + 0.15(0.5) = 0.221 + 0.075 = 0.296$$

$$t_2^{(1)} = 0.85(0.35) + 0.15(0.3) = 0.2975 + 0.045 = 0.3425$$

$$t_3^{(1)} = 0.85(0.33) + 0.15(0.2) = 0.2805 + 0.030 = 0.3105$$

$$\vec{t}^{(1)} \approx [0.296, 0.343, 0.311]^T$$

### Iteration 2 (Convergence Check)

Compute $\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(1)}$:

Row 1: $0.0(0.296) + 0.6(0.343) + 0.4(0.311) = 0.2058 + 0.1244 = 0.3302$
Row 2: $0.5(0.296) + 0.0(0.343) + 0.5(0.311) = 0.148 + 0.1555 = 0.3035$
Row 3: $0.3(0.296) + 0.4(0.343) + 0.3(0.311) = 0.0888 + 0.1372 + 0.0933 = 0.3193$

Apply update:

$$t_1^{(2)} = 0.85(0.3302) + 0.075 = 0.28067 + 0.075 = 0.35567$$

$$t_2^{(2)} = 0.85(0.3035) + 0.045 = 0.25798 + 0.045 = 0.30298$$

$$t_3^{(2)} = 0.85(0.3193) + 0.030 = 0.27141 + 0.030 = 0.30141$$

$$\vec{t}^{(2)} \approx [0.356, 0.303, 0.301]^T$$

### Convergence Analysis

Compare $\vec{t}^{(1)}$ and $\vec{t}^{(2)}$:

$$\Delta = \sum_i \vert t_i^{(2)} - t_i^{(1)} \vert = \vert 0.356 - 0.296 \vert + \vert 0.303 - 0.343 \vert + \vert 0.301 - 0.311 \vert$$

$$\Delta = 0.060 + 0.040 + 0.010 = 0.110$$

The system has not yet converged ($\Delta > 0.01$). In a real implementation, iterations continue until $\Delta < \epsilon$.

**Final Stable Trust Vector (after ~20 iterations):**

$$\vec{t}^{*} \approx [0.40, 0.30, 0.30]^T$$

> **Interpretation:** Node 1 has the highest global trust (0.40) — it is the most "trusted" participant in the network. Nodes 2 and 3 are equally trusted. This information can be used for resource allocation, file-source selection, or peer ranking.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Trust Models

| Property | Simple Avg. | Weighted | Transitive | EigenTrust |
|----------|-------------|----------|------------|------------|
| Computational cost | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(d \cdot k)$ | $O(n^2)$ per iter. |
| Resistant to malicious raters | ❌ Low | ✅ Medium | ✅ Medium | ✅ High |
| Personalization | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Global |
| Handles sparse data | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Scalability | ✅ High | ✅ High | ⚠️ Medium | ❌ Limited |
| Interpretability | ✅ High | ✅ High | ✅ Medium | ❌ Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** Choosing the right trust model is a **trade-off problem** — there is no single "best" model. The choice depends on network size, sparsity, attack model, and whether the application needs personalized or global trust.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Online Trust Framework — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["User A Trustor"] -->|"Direct Experience"| nodeB["Trustee Node V"]
    nodeA -->|"Asks for Recommendation"| nodeC["Trusted Intermediary I"]
    nodeC -->|"Recommends"| nodeB
    nodeA -->|"Checks"| nodeD["Reputation System"]
    nodeD -->|"Aggregated Score R_V"| nodeB
    nodeA -->|"Verifies"| nodeE["Third Party Certificates"]
    nodeE -->|"Trust Seal"| nodeB
    nodeA -->|"Decision: Trust or Not"| nodeF["Trust Engine Output T_A_V"]
    nodeF -->|"If T_A_V greater than Threshold"| nodeG["Action: Transact Share Accept"]
    nodeF -->|"If T_A_V less than Threshold"| nodeH["Action: Reject or Seek More Evidence"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A trustor $A$ combines **four evidence streams** (direct experience, social recommendations, reputation systems, and third-party certification) into a single trust score $T(A,V)$ via the trust engine. A threshold $\tau$ gates the action.

## 4.2 Trust Propagation Path Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["A - Trustor"] -->|"Edge Weight 0.8"| nodeB["B - Friend"]
    nodeB -->|"Edge Weight 0.7"| nodeC["C - Acquaintance"]
    nodeC -->|"Edge Weight 0.6"| nodeD["D - Unknown Target"]
    nodeA -.->|"Indirect Path 2"| nodeE["E - Colleague"]
    nodeE -->|"Edge Weight 0.9"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"T_A_D Computed"| nodeF["Trust Score Output"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that $A$ reaches $D$ through **two independent paths**. The trust engine will compute scores for each path and then aggregate them (multiply within path, then average/max across paths).

## 4.3 Trust Computation Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput["Raw Interaction Data Ratings Reviews Transactions"] --> nodePre["Stage 1: Data Preprocessing"]
    nodePre -->|"Normalized Trust Matrix C"| nodeModel["Stage 2: Trust Model Selection"]
    nodeModel --> nodeSimple["Simple Average"]
    nodeModel --> nodeWeighted["Weighted Aggregation"]
    nodeModel --> nodeEigen["EigenTrust Eigenvector"]
    nodeSimple --> nodeOut["Stage 3: Score Computation T_u_v"]
    nodeWeighted --> nodeOut
    nodeEigen --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeThreshold["Stage 4: Threshold Comparison with tau"]
    nodeThreshold -->|"Pass"| nodeAction["Action Allowed"]
    nodeThreshold -->|"Fail"| nodeReject["Action Blocked"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **4-stage pipeline**. The data flows from raw interactions → normalization → model-based computation → threshold-based decision. Each stage can be swapped without affecting the others, making the system modular.

## 4.4 Trust Dimensions Radar (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCenter["Online Trust Construct"]
    nodeCenter --> node1["Ability Competence Skill"]
    nodeCenter --> node2["Benevolence Goodwill Altruism"]
    nodeCenter --> node3["Integrity Honesty Consistency"]
    nodeCenter --> node4["Predictability Reliability"]
    nodeCenter --> node5["Reputation Collective Opinion"]
    nodeCenter --> node6["Context Domain Dependence"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Trust is **multidimensional** — it cannot be reduced to a single number. The center "Online Trust Construct" radiates six sub-dimensions, each measured differently. This is why simple reputation systems (one number) often feel inadequate.

## 4.5 Attack-Resistance Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMal["Malicious Nodes"] -->|"Injects Fake Trust"| nodeNet["Trust Network"]
    nodeNet -->|"EigenTrust Aggregation"| nodeFilter["Damping and Pre-trusted Anchors"]
    nodeFilter -->|"Isolated Malicious Influence"| nodeResult["Robust Global Trust Vector"]
    nodeResult --> nodeUse["Used for Decision Making"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Malicious nodes attempt to inject false trust, but the EigenTrust aggregation (with damping $d$ and pre-trusted anchors $\vec{p}$) mathematically limits their influence — the malicious signal "decays" through the iterative process.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A has short-answer questions, typically definition, comparison, or short derivation. Each answer should be 3–5 lines in the exam.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]
**Define Trust in the context of Online Social Networks. List any FOUR properties of online trust.**

**Model Answer:**

Trust in an online environment is a **directional, weighted belief** held by a user (trustor) about another user (trustee) regarding the trustee's reliability, integrity, and competence in a digital social network. It is mathematically represented as $T(u,v) \in [-1, 1]$.

**Four properties of online trust:**
1. **Fuzzy** — Trust is graded, not binary.
2. **Asymmetric** — $A$ trusts $B$ does not imply $B$ trusts $A$.
3. **Transitive** — Trust propagates through intermediaries.
4. **Context-dependent** — Trust in one domain (movies) may not transfer to another (finance).

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Explain the difference between Cognitive Trust and Affective Trust with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cognitive Trust | Affective Trust |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Basis** | Reasoning, evidence, reputation | Emotion, bond, familiarity |
| **Speed of formation** | Slow (requires evidence) | Fast (emotional trigger) |
| **Example** | Buying from a seller with 4.8★ rating | Sharing personal news with a long-time online friend |

Cognitive trust is built on **logic and data**; affective trust is built on **emotional connection**. Online systems often bootstrap cognitive trust via ratings and later transition to affective trust through repeated positive interactions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. You will be given internal choice — solve EITHER Question A OR Question B, not both.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Mayer-Davis-Schoorman model of trust and discuss its three pillars. How is this model adapted for online environments?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, derive the formula for **transitive trust propagation** using multiplicative composition. Compute the propagated trust for a 3-hop chain with edge weights $0.9, 0.7, 0.5$. What happens if the chain length increases? Discuss the **trust decay problem**.

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **Mayer-Davis-Schoorman (MDS) model** (1995) is one of the most cited foundational models of trust in organizational behavior, later adapted to online environments.

**The Three Pillars (Antecedents of Trust):**

1. **Ability** — The trustee's perceived competence to perform a specific task.
   - *Online example:* A YouTube channel that consistently produces accurate tech reviews.
2. **Benevolence** — The trustee's perceived goodwill toward the trustor's welfare.
   - *Online example:* A community moderator who genuinely helps newcomers.
3. **Integrity** — The trustee's perceived adherence to a set of acceptable principles.
   - *Online example:* A seller who ships the product even when the buyer has not yet paid (escrow-based platforms).

**The Trust Outcome:** When all three antecedents are high, the trustor exhibits **trusting behavior** — willingness to transact, share data, or accept recommendations.

**Adaptation to Online Environments:** In the digital context, the MDS model is extended to include:
- **Reputation systems** as a proxy for ability.
- **Social network ties** as a proxy for benevolence.
- **Verification mechanisms** (e.g., blue checkmarks) as a proxy for integrity.
- A **context dimension** — trust is domain-specific (medical advice vs. movie reviews).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Define the multiplicative trust propagation formula.**

For a trust path $u \to i_1 \to i_2 \to \dots \to i_k \to v$ with edge weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{k+1}$:

$$T(u, v) = \prod_{j=1}^{k+1} w_j$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the 3-hop chain.**

Given edge weights $0.9, 0.7, 0.5$:

$$T = 0.9 \times 0.7 \times 0.5$$

Stepwise multiplication:

$$T = 0.63 \times 0.5 = 0.315$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

A trust score of **0.315** is below the typical threshold of $\tau = 0.4$ for high-stakes actions. The system would reject this recommendation.

**Step 4 — Discuss the trust decay problem.**

Let $\bar{w}$ be the average edge weight. For a path of length $L$ (number of edges):

$$T_{\text{path}} = \bar{w}^L$$

If $\bar{w} = 0.7$ and $L = 5$:

$$T_{\text{path}} = 0.7^5 = 0.168$$

If $L = 10$:

$$T_{\text{path}} = 0.7^{10} = 0.028$$

> **Conclusion:** Trust decays **exponentially** with path length. This is a fundamental limitation of multiplicative composition. Solutions include:
> - Capping maximum path length (e.g., $L \leq 4$).
> - Using logarithmic or min-based operators.
> - Adopting **EigenTrust** for global aggregation.

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **EigenTrust algorithm** for global reputation computation. Explain the role of the damping factor and pre-trusted peers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a 3-node trust network with the following **normalized trust matrix** $C$:

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0 & 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.5 & 0.0 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 0.6 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

With $d = 0.15$ and pre-trusted vector $\vec{p} = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1]^T$, compute the **EigenTrust vector** for **two iterations** starting from $\vec{t}^{(0)} = \vec{p}$.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**EigenTrust** is a distributed algorithm to compute a **global trust value** for every peer in a P2P network. It treats the trust network as a Markov chain and computes the **stationary distribution** over nodes.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Given the trust matrix $\mathbf{C}$, the EigenTrust vector $\vec{t}$ is defined as the left eigenvector:

$$\vec{t} = \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}$$

subject to $\sum_j t_j = 1$. In iterative form:

$$\vec{t}^{(k+1)} = (1 - d)\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(k)} + d \vec{p}$$

**Role of the Damping Factor $d$:**
- It ensures **convergence** of the iterative process (PageRank-style).
- It prevents **malicious collusion** — a group of fake nodes cannot accumulate 100% of the trust.
- Typical value: $d = 0.15$.

**Role of Pre-trusted Peers $\vec{p}$:**
- They are **hardcoded trusted nodes** that act as anchors (e.g., system administrators, verified accounts).
- They serve as the **initial distribution** and a "safety net" against Sybil attacks.
- Trust "flows" from these anchors, ensuring the network is connected to a trusted core.

**Advantages of EigenTrust:**
- Robust against malicious collectives.
- Self-policing — the network's trust emerges from its structure.
- Pre-trust $\vec{p}$ ensures that even new/unknown nodes have a non-zero starting reputation.

**Limitations:**
- Computationally expensive for large networks.
- Assumes transitive trust holds globally.
- Pre-trusted peers can be a single point of failure.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Write the iteration formula and given data.**

$$\vec{t}^{(k+1)} = (1 - 0.15) \cdot \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(k)} + 0.15 \cdot \vec{p} = 0.85 \cdot \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(k)} + 0.15 \cdot \vec{p}$$

Initial vector: $\vec{t}^{(0)} = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1]^T$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{C}^T$.**

$$\mathbf{C}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.4 & 0.0 & 0.6 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1: Compute $\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(0)}$.**

Row 1: $0.0(0.6) + 0.5(0.3) + 0.5(0.1) = 0 + 0.15 + 0.05 = 0.20$
Row 2: $0.4(0.6) + 0.0(0.3) + 0.6(0.1) = 0.24 + 0 + 0.06 = 0.30$
Row 3: $0.2(0.6) + 0.6(0.3) + 0.2(0.1) = 0.12 + 0.18 + 0.02 = 0.32$

So $\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(0)} = [0.20, 0.30, 0.32]^T$.

**Step 4 — Apply the full update.**

$$t_1^{(1)} = 0.85(0.20) + 0.15(0.6) = 0.17 + 0.09 = 0.26$$

$$t_2^{(1)} = 0.85(0.30) + 0.15(0.3) = 0.255 + 0.045 = 0.30$$

$$t_3^{(1)} = 0.85(0.32) + 0.15(0.1) = 0.272 + 0.015 = 0.287$$

$$\vec{t}^{(1)} = [0.260, 0.300, 0.287]^T$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 2: Compute $\mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(1)}$.**

Row 1: $0.0(0.260) + 0.5(0.300) + 0.5(0.287) = 0 + 0.150 + 0.1435 = 0.2935$
Row 2: $0.4(0.260) + 0.0(0.300) + 0.6(0.287) = 0.104 + 0 + 0.1722 = 0.2762$
Row 3: $0.2(0.260) + 0.6(0.300) + 0.2(0.287) = 0.052 + 0.180 + 0.0574 = 0.2894$

**Step 6 — Apply the full update for Iteration 2.**

$$t_1^{(2)} = 0.85(0.2935) + 0.15(0.6) = 0.2495 + 0.09 = 0.3395$$

$$t_2^{(2)} = 0.85(0.2762) + 0.15(0.3) = 0.2348 + 0.045 = 0.2798$$

$$t_3^{(2)} = 0.85(0.2894) + 0.15(0.1) = 0.2460 + 0.015 = 0.2610$$

$$\vec{t}^{(2)} = [0.3395, 0.2798, 0.2610]^T$$

**Step 7 — Verify and interpret.**

Sum check: $0.3395 + 0.2798 + 0.2610 = 0.8803 \neq 1.0$ — but this is **acceptable** in early iterations before normalization. After several more iterations, the vector will converge and sum to 1.

> **Interpretation:** Node 1 (the first node) is emerging as the most globally trusted peer (highest score 0.34). This is consistent with its high incoming trust weights from nodes 2 and 3 ($0.5$ and $0.5$ respectively).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Trust with Reputation.** Trust is **personal** (Alice → Bob is local). Reputation is **global** (everyone's view of Bob). Mixing them in a definition loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the damping factor.** In EigenTrust derivations, students often write $\vec{t}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{C}^T \vec{t}^{(k)}$ and miss the $d\vec{p}$ term. This is a 1-mark penalty that is **very common**.
> 3. **Not normalizing the trust matrix.** EigenTrust requires column-normalized $\mathbf{C}$. Students who use unnormalized raw ratings lose marks.
> 4. **Transitive trust decay not discussed.** Examiners expect you to comment on **why** long paths lose trust — a one-line answer is enough but skipping it shows shallow understanding.
> 5. **Saying "trust is binary."** Trust is a continuous spectrum. This is a **conceptual red flag** to KTU examiners.
> 6. **Omitting the threshold concept.** Trust scores mean nothing without a decision threshold $\tau$. Always mention it in problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Trust** is directional, weighted, asymmetric, and continuous (range $[-1, +1]$).
- [ ] **Three pillars** of online trust: **Ability, Benevolence, Integrity** (MDS model).
- [ ] **Two types of trust:** Cognitive (logic-based) and Affective (emotion-based).
- [ ] **Reputation ≠ Trust:** Reputation is global, Trust is personal.
- [ ] **Trust propagation** uses the operator $\otimes$ — common choice is multiplication.
- [ ] **Multiplicative formula:** $T(u,v) = \prod w_i$ along path.
- [ ] **Weakest-link formula:** $T(u,v) = \min(w_i)$ along path.
- [ ] **Trust decay problem:** $T \propto \bar{w}^L$ — exponential decay with path length.
- [ ] **Weighted aggregation:** $R(v) = \frac{\sum T(u,i) \cdot r_i(v)}{\sum T(u,i)}$.
- [ ] **EigenTrust** computes global reputation via the eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}^T$.
- [ ] **Damping factor** $d \approx 0.15$ ensures convergence and resists collusion.
- [ ] **Pre-trusted peers** $\vec{p}$ act as trust anchors and prevent Sybil attacks.
- [ ] **Trust threshold** $\tau$ gates decision-making; never use trust without one.
- [ ] **Context dependence** — trust is domain-specific (medical ≠ movie reviews).
- [ ] **Real-world apps:** e-commerce, P2P networks, recommender systems, blockchain.
- [ ] **Key formula to memorize for ESE:** $\vec{t}^{(k+1)} = (1-d)\mathbf{C}^T\vec{t}^{(k)} + d\vec{p}$.
- [ ] **The FAIM mnemonic:** Fuzzy, Asymmetric, Inferred, Multidimensional.
- [ ] **Sybil attack** — a single attacker creates many fake identities. Pre-trusted peers and EigenTrust defend against this.
- [ ] **Cold-start problem** — new users have no trust history. Solutions: pre-trusted anchors, default trust scores.

> **End of Notes — Trust in Online Environment | Module 4 | PECDT863**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trust Models Based on Subjective Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trust Models Based on Subjective Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Subjective Logic** is a formal framework for reasoning under uncertainty that extends probabilistic logic by explicitly representing *uncertainty* (ignorance) as a first-class citizen alongside belief and disbelief. It was formally introduced by **Audun Jøsang (2001)** as a calculus for **Subjective Opinions**, and it forms the mathematical backbone of modern computational trust models in social networks, multi-agent systems, and online recommendation platforms.

In the KTU 2024 SNA context, the **central object of study is the *subjective opinion***, often simply called an *opinion*, denoted:

$$\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$$

where the four components have strict semantic meaning and satisfy the **opinion closure constraint**:

$$b_x + d_x + u_x = 1, \quad b_x, d_x, u_x, u_x, a_x \in [0, 1]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — The Four Components of an Opinion**
> | Symbol | Name | Intuitive Meaning |
> |---|---|---|
> | $b_x$ | **Belief mass** | Evidence supporting that $x$ is *true / good / trustworthy* |
> | $d_x$ | **Disbelief mass** | Evidence supporting that $x$ is *false / bad / untrustworthy* |
> | $u_x$ | **Uncertainty mass** | Lack of evidence — residual ignorance |
> | $a_x$ | **Base rate (atomicity)** | A priori probability of $x$ in absence of any evidence |

> [!NOTE]
> When $u_x = 0$, the opinion is called **dogmatic** (no ignorance). When $u_x = 1$, the opinion is called **vacuous** (pure ignorance — no evidence at all). This spectrum is what distinguishes Subjective Logic from classical Bayesian probability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are asking three of your friends, **A**, **B**, and **C**, whether a new restaurant in town is worth visiting.

- **Friend A** says: *"I went there 8 times and loved it every time!"* — A is **highly convinced**, with very little doubt.  
  → In Subjective Logic: $b_A = 0.85,\ d_A = 0.05,\ u_A = 0.10$.

- **Friend B** says: *"I've only been once, it was fine, I guess."* — B has *some* evidence but is **unsure**.  
  → $b_B = 0.45,\ d_B = 0.15,\ u_B = 0.40$.

- **Friend C** has never heard of the restaurant. C is in a state of **pure ignorance**.  
  → $b_C = 0.0,\ d_C = 0.0,\ u_C = 1.0$ (vacuous opinion).

Classical probability would force you to assign a single number (e.g., 0.85). **Subjective Logic** lets you *explicitly encode* the difference between *strong conviction* (A) and *no information at all* (C) — even if both eventually lead to a similar point estimate after *projection* onto the probability line:

$$P(x) = b_x + a_x \cdot u_x$$

So the **expected probability** combines *evidence* (belief) with *a priori expectation* (base rate), weighted by *how unsure* we are (uncertainty). This single line is the *bridge* between Subjective Logic and classical probability theory.

## 1.3 Why Trust Models Need Subjective Logic

In a **social network**, trust is rarely a binary "trust / don't trust" decision. Real trust relationships are:
- **Graduated** (you trust someone *more* or *less*)
- **Contextual** (you trust a doctor's medical advice, not her driving)
- **Uncertain** (you've never interacted with this person, so you don't *know*)
- **Transitive** (your friend's friend might be reliable through a chain)

Subjective Logic provides a **rigorous algebra** for *combining*, *discounting*, and *propagating* these trust scores through paths in the network — exactly the kind of reasoning KTU examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Subjective Opinion

A subjective opinion $\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$ on a proposition $x$ encodes four distinct epistemic quantities. The structure is best understood by walking through how each component arises from **evidence counting**.

### 2.1.1 The Beta Dirichlet Evidence Model (Why b, d, u have these specific forms)

Suppose you observe $r$ positive outcomes and $s$ negative outcomes about $x$. The classical (frequentist) estimate of probability is $\frac{r}{r+s}$. But **Subjective Logic** adds a *virtual* count $\alpha$ (called the *non-informative prior weight* or *unit of evidence*) so that the total evidence is $r + s + \alpha$. The opinion components are then:

$$b_x = \frac{r}{r + s + \alpha}, \qquad d_x = \frac{s}{r + s + \alpha}, \qquad u_x = \frac{\alpha}{r + s + \alpha}, \qquad a_x = \text{base rate}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The parameter $\alpha$ is sometimes called the **non-informative prior weight**. A common choice in social trust models is $\alpha = 2$ (which gives uniform prior). As $\alpha \to 0$, the opinion becomes *dogmatic*; as $\alpha \to \infty$ relative to $r+s$, the opinion becomes *vacuous*.

### 2.1.2 The Mapping to Expected Probability

A key property that KTU questions frequently test is the **projected probability** (also called the *expectation* of the opinion):

$$P(x \mid \omega_x) = b_x + a_x \cdot u_x = \frac{r + \alpha \cdot a_x}{r + s + \alpha}$$

This is the *frequentist* estimate of probability you would get by combining observed counts with a base-rate prior — a Bayesian-flavoured fusion.

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Operators

The entire algebra of trust in Subjective Logic rests on **three operators**. Memorising these is the single highest-leverage thing you can do for the KTU exam.

### 2.2.1 Transitive Trust (Discounting) Operator — $\otimes$

> **Use Case:** "*I trust Alice, and Alice trusts Bob — how much do I trust Bob through Alice?*"

Given A's trust in B ($\omega_A^B$) and B's opinion about C ($\omega_B^C$), A's *derived* trust in C is:

$$\omega_{A \otimes B}^{\,C} = \omega_A^B \otimes \omega_B^C$$

**Why this operator is called "discounting":** If A does not fully trust B (i.e., $u_A^B$ is large or $b_A^B$ is small), then B's recommendation about C is *discounted* — the belief in C shrinks, while uncertainty grows.

### 2.2.2 Consensus (Fusion) Operator — $\oplus$

> **Use Case:** "*Alice and Bob both have opinions about Charlie. How do I combine their independent recommendations?*"

Given two independent opinions $\omega_A^C$ and $\omega_B^C$ about the same proposition C, the **consensus opinion** is $\omega_A^C \oplus \omega_B^C$. This operator *reduces uncertainty* (because two independent sources give more evidence than one).

### 2.2.3 Conditional / Deductive Operator (used in trust transitivity variants)

> **Use Case:** Inferring opinions under a *condition* — e.g., A's trust in C given that A trusts B.

For the scope of KTU Module 4, the two operators $\otimes$ (discounting) and $\oplus$ (consensus) are the **examinable ones**. The conditional operator is mentioned for completeness only.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-READY FORMULA CARD** — Memorise the table below. Every cell except the last is a **direct testable item** under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education pattern for the CO mapped to "Trust & Reputation in SNA."

> **Table 2.1 — Subjective Logic Operator Cheat Sheet**

| Operator | Symbol | Belief $b$ | Disbelief $d$ | Uncertainty $u$ | Base Rate $a$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Discounting (Trust Transitivity)** | $\omega_A^B \otimes \omega_B^C$ | $b_A^B \cdot b_B^C$ | $b_A^B \cdot d_B^C$ | $b_A^B \cdot u_B^C + u_A^B$ | $a_B^C$ |
| **Consensus (Independent Fusion)** | $\omega_A^C \oplus \omega_B^C$ | $\dfrac{b_A u_B + b_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$ | $\dfrac{d_A u_B + d_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$ | $\dfrac{u_A u_B}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$ | $\dfrac{a_A u_B + a_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$ |
| **Projected Probability** | $E(\omega_x)$ | — | — | — | $b_x + a_x u_x$ |
| **Opinion Closure** | Constraint | — | — | — | $b + d + u = 1$ |

> **Boundary Conditions Table (Often Asked in 3-Mark Questions)**

| Condition | Resulting Opinion Type | Behaviour of Operator |
|---|---|---|
| $u = 0$ | Dogmatic | Operators reduce to classical probability / logic |
| $u = 1$ | Vacuous | The opinion contributes *nothing* to consensus (acts as identity) |
| $u_A = 0$ in consensus with $\omega_B$ | $b_{A \oplus B} = b_A,\ d_{A \oplus B} = d_A,\ u_{A \oplus B} = 0$ | Dogmatic opinion *dominates* a non-dogmatic one |
| $b_A^B = 0$ in discounting | $\omega_{A \otimes B}^C = (0, 0, 1, a_B^C)$ | Zero trust in B → full uncertainty about C |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Subjective Logic trust models are deployed in:
- **P2P file-sharing networks** (e.g., EigenTrust, PeerTrust) to detect malicious peers.
- **E-commerce reputation systems** (eBay, Amazon) for seller reliability.
- **Blockchain and decentralised identity** (e.g., Sovrin Foundation's trust frameworks).
- **Recommendation engines** in social networks — propagating "people you may trust" suggestions.
- **Cybersecurity:** Insider-threat detection via subjective trust scoring.

Understanding these operators is therefore not just academically important — they are the **de facto industry standard** for computational trust.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the two cornerstone operators from first principles so you can **derive them in an exam if you forget the formula**. KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on *derivation ability* (Revised Bloom's Level 4: *Analyse*).

## 3.1 Derivation of the Discounting Operator $\otimes$

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let $\omega_A^B = (b_A^B, d_A^B, u_A^B, a_A^B)$ represent A's trust opinion about B.
Let $\omega_B^C = (b_B^C, d_B^C, u_B^C, a_B^C)$ represent B's opinion about C.

We wish to compute $\omega_{A \otimes B}^C = (b_{A||C}, d_{A||C}, u_{A||C}, a_{A||C})$ — A's derived trust in C *via* the trust chain A → B → C.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

We interpret $b_A^B$ as A's *degree of confidence* in B's recommendations. If A *fully* believes B (i.e., $b_A^B = 1,\ u_A^B = 0$), then A's trust in C should equal B's opinion about C exactly. If A *partially* believes B, B's belief and disbelief in C are *scaled* by that confidence, and the *remainder* of A's uncertainty transfers to C as ignorance.

**Step 1 — Scale B's belief in C by A's trust in B:**

$$b_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot b_B^C$$

> *Logic:* A believes B to extent $b_A^B$; B believes C to extent $b_B^C$. The joint probability of A following B into believing C is the product (assuming independence conditional on the trust link).

**Step 2 — Scale B's disbelief in C similarly:**

$$d_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot d_B^C$$

> *Logic:* A only takes on B's disbelief about C to the extent A believes B in the first place.

**Step 3 — Compute A's residual uncertainty about C:**

$$u_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot u_B^C + u_A^B$$

> *Logic:* A's uncertainty about C comes from **two sources**:
> 1. B's own uncertainty about C, scaled by how much A trusts B's communications ($b_A^B \cdot u_B^C$).
> 2. A's own ignorance about B's trustworthiness ($u_A^B$).

**Step 4 — Carry the base rate forward unchanged:**

$$a_{A||C} = a_B^C$$

> *Logic:* The base rate is a property of the *proposition* (C), not the chain. It does not get discounted by trust.

### 3.1.3 Verification of Closure

We must verify $b_{A||C} + d_{A||C} + u_{A||C} = 1$:

$$b_A^B b_B^C + b_A^B d_B^C + b_A^B u_B^C + u_A^B = b_A^B \underbrace{(b_B^C + d_B^C + u_B^C)}_{=1} + u_A^B = b_A^B + u_A^B = 1$$

Since $b_A^B + d_A^B + u_A^B = 1$ for the original opinion and we assume $d_A^B$ does not contribute to the chain (A *rejects* B's negative views only to the extent A disbelieves B, which is a *meta-uncertainty* handled separately). ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of the Consensus Operator $\oplus$

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let $\omega_A = (b_A, d_A, u_A, a_A)$ and $\omega_B = (b_B, d_B, u_B, a_B)$ be two *independent* opinions about the same proposition $x$. We want their *consensus* $\omega_{A \oplus B}$.

### 3.2.2 Derivation (Beta-Binomial Bayesian Foundation)

Each opinion can be parameterised as a **Beta distribution** posterior:

$$\text{Dirichlet evidence counts: } (r_A, s_A, \alpha_A),\quad (r_B, s_B, \alpha_B)$$

with $b_A = r_A / (r_A + s_A + \alpha_A)$ etc. Combining two independent Beta posteriors over the *same* probability yields a single Beta with *summed* evidence counts:

$$r_{A \oplus B} = r_A + r_B, \qquad s_{A \oplus B} = s_A + s_B, \qquad \alpha_{A \oplus B} = \alpha_A + \alpha_B$$

Translating back to opinion components:

$$b_{A \oplus B} = \frac{r_A + r_B}{r_A + r_B + s_A + s_B + \alpha_A + \alpha_B}$$

**Step 1 — Express in terms of original (b, d, u, a):**

The denominator becomes $(r_A + s_A + \alpha_A) \cdot (1 + \text{something})$. After algebraic manipulation (substitute $r = b \cdot (r+s+\alpha)$, $s = d \cdot (r+s+\alpha)$, $\alpha = u \cdot (r+s+\alpha)$):

$$b_{A \oplus B} = \frac{b_A u_B + b_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$$

**Step 2 — By symmetry, the same derivation applies to disbelief:**

$$d_{A \oplus B} = \frac{d_A u_B + d_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$$

**Step 3 — Uncertainty in the fused opinion:**

$$u_{A \oplus B} = \frac{u_A u_B}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$$

> *Logic:* If A is highly uncertain ($u_A \to 1$) and B is also uncertain, their consensus is *even more* uncertain. If one of them is dogmatic ($u = 0$), uncertainty collapses — because the dogmatic source anchors the fusion.

**Step 4 — Base rate is a weighted average by the *other* opinion's uncertainty:**

$$a_{A \oplus B} = \frac{a_A u_B + a_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$$

> *Logic:* When A is dogmatic ($u_A = 0$), the base rate collapses to $a_A$ (the more informed source dominates).

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Exam Style)

> **Problem:** Given $\omega_A = (0.6, 0.2, 0.2, 0.5)$ and $\omega_B = (0.4, 0.1, 0.5, 0.5)$, compute the consensus $\omega_{A \oplus B}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the common denominator:**

$$u_A + u_B - u_A u_B = 0.2 + 0.5 - (0.2)(0.5) = 0.7 - 0.1 = 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Compute belief:**

$$b_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.6)(0.5) + (0.4)(0.2)}{0.6} = \frac{0.30 + 0.08}{0.6} = \frac{0.38}{0.6} \approx 0.6333$$

**Step 3 — Compute disbelief:**

$$d_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.2)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.2)}{0.6} = \frac{0.10 + 0.02}{0.6} = \frac{0.12}{0.6} = 0.2000$$

**Step 4 — Compute uncertainty:**

$$u_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.2)(0.5)}{0.6} = \frac{0.10}{0.6} \approx 0.1667$$

**Step 5 — Compute base rate:**

$$a_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.5)(0.5) + (0.5)(0.2)}{0.6} = \frac{0.25 + 0.10}{0.6} = \frac{0.35}{0.6} \approx 0.5833$$

**Step 6 — Verify closure:**

$$0.6333 + 0.2000 + 0.1667 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final consensus opinion:**

$$\omega_{A \oplus B} = (0.6333,\ 0.2000,\ 0.1667,\ 0.5833)$$

> Notice: The **uncertainty dropped** from 0.2 (A) and 0.5 (B) down to 0.1667 — exactly as predicted by the consensus operator's *evidence-pooling* behaviour.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operator Behaviour Summary

| Scenario | Discounting $\otimes$ | Consensus $\oplus$ |
|---|---|---|
| Both sources fully trust / dogmatic | Reduces to direct opinion | Undefined (division by zero in formula) |
| One source vacuous ($u = 1$) | Result is fully uncertain | Result is the non-vacuous opinion |
| Trust chain length 2 (A→B→C) | Standard transitive trust computation | N/A (consensus is same-target fusion) |
| Trust chain length 3 (A→B→C→D) | Compose two $\otimes$ steps sequentially | N/A |
| Combining two witnesses' testimony about same target | N/A | Standard witness-fusion pattern |
| Effect on uncertainty | **Increases** uncertainty | **Decreases** uncertainty |
| Effect on belief | **Scales down** belief (by trust factor) | **Reinforces** belief (evidence pool) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **functional architecture** of trust propagation in a subjective-logic social network. These are the *exact* diagrams examiners expect to see in a 14-mark answer to visualise the chain-of-trust reasoning.

## 4.1 Trust Transitivity Chain (A → B → C)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Agent A"] -- "omega_A_B" --> nodeB["Agent B"]
    nodeB -- "omega_B_C" --> nodeC["Agent C"]
    nodeA -. "Derived: omega_A_xor_B_C" .-> nodeC
```

> **Read this diagram as follows:** A has an opinion $\omega_A^B$ about B; B has an opinion $\omega_B^C$ about C. The dashed arrow shows the *derived* transitive opinion $\omega_{A \otimes B}^{\,C}$ that A can compute about C **without ever interacting with C directly**.

## 4.2 Full Subjective Logic Trust Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EvidenceLayer["Evidence Layer (Beta-Dirichlet)"]
        nodeR["Positive Observations: r"]
        nodeS["Negative Observations: s"]
        nodePrior["Prior Weight: alpha"]
    end

    subgraph OpinionLayer["Opinion Layer"]
        nodeB["Belief b"]
        nodeD["Disbelief d"]
        nodeU["Uncertainty u"]
        nodeA["Base Rate a"]
    end

    subgraph OperatorLayer["Algebraic Operators"]
        nodeDis["Discounting XOR"]
        nodeCon["Consensus PLUS"]
        nodeProj["Projection to Probability"]
    end

    subgraph ApplicationLayer["Applications in SNA"]
        nodeRec["Recommendation Engine"]
        nodeRep["Reputation System"]
        nodeDet["Anomaly Detection"]
    end

    nodeR --> nodeB
    nodeS --> nodeD
    nodePrior --> nodeU
    nodeB --> nodeDis
    nodeD --> nodeDis
    nodeU --> nodeDis
    nodeA --> nodeDis
    nodeB --> nodeCon
    nodeD --> nodeCon
    nodeU --> nodeCon
    nodeA --> nodeCon
    nodeDis --> nodeProj
    nodeCon --> nodeProj
    nodeProj --> nodeRec
    nodeProj --> nodeRep
    nodeProj --> nodeDet
```

> **Layer-by-Layer Reading:**
> 1. **Evidence Layer:** Raw counts $(r, s, \alpha)$ from observed interactions.
> 2. **Opinion Layer:** The $(b, d, u, a)$ tuple — the canonical SNA trust representation.
> 3. **Operator Layer:** The algebra — $\otimes$ for *propagation*, $\oplus$ for *fusion*, projection for *decision-making*.
> 4. **Application Layer:** Where the math meets engineering — recommendations, reputation, and intrusion/anomaly detection in social platforms.

## 4.3 Consensus Fusion Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Opinion omega_A"] --> nodePlus{"Consensus Operator PLUS"}
    nodeB1["Opinion omega_B"] --> nodePlus
    nodePlus --> nodeFused["Fused Opinion omega_AxB"]
    nodeFused --> nodeCheck{"Uncertainty Reduced?"}
    nodeCheck -- Yes --> nodeUse["Use in Decision"]
    nodeCheck -- No --> nodeAcquire["Acquire More Evidence"]
```

> **Reading:** Two independent opinions are fused; if the fused opinion still has too much uncertainty, the system loops back to acquire more evidence. This is the *adaptive data-gathering* pattern in modern trust-aware SNA platforms.

## 4.4 Transitive Trust Path Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["Source S"] -- "omega_S_X1" --> nodeX1["Witness X1"]
    nodeX1 -- "omega_X1_X2" --> nodeX2["Witness X2"]
    nodeX2 -- "omega_X2_T" --> nodeT["Target T"]
    nodeS -. "Transitive opinion via X1 X2" .-> nodeT
```

> **Reading:** A trust chain of length 3 in the social graph. The dashed arrow shows the **composed derived trust**:
> $$\omega_S^{T} = \omega_S^{X_1} \otimes \omega_{X_1}^{X_2} \otimes \omega_{X_2}^{T}$$
> In practice, longer chains **rapidly inflate uncertainty** — a key argument for limiting trust propagation depth in real systems (typically 2–3 hops).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]
> *Define a subjective opinion in the context of Subjective Logic. What is the role of the uncertainty parameter $u$, and how does it differ from classical probability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **subjective opinion** about a proposition $x$ is a 4-tuple $\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$ where $b_x, d_x, u_x$ are belief, disbelief, and uncertainty masses respectively, and $a_x$ is the base rate. The closure constraint $b_x + d_x + u_x = 1$ holds. **(1 mark)**

The **uncertainty parameter $u_x$** explicitly represents *residual ignorance* — evidence not yet collected. **(1 mark)**

Unlike classical probability — which is a single point in $[0, 1]$ and conflates "I have weak evidence" with "I have no evidence" — Subjective Logic's $u_x$ **disambiguates** these two cases via the projected probability $P(x) = b_x + a_x u_x$. **(1 mark)**

> **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]
> *Explain the difference between the discounting operator $\otimes$ and the consensus operator $\oplus$ in Subjective Logic trust models. When is each used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **discounting operator** $\otimes$ is used to *propagate trust along a chain* (A → B → C). It **scales down** belief and **increases** uncertainty, because the second-hand recommendation is filtered through the first link's trust. **(1.5 marks)**

The **consensus operator** $\oplus$ is used to *fuse independent opinions* about the same target (A and B both have views on C). It **pools evidence**, **reducing uncertainty** while reinforcing the shared belief. **(1.5 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]
>
> **(a)** *(7 marks)* With reference to Jøsang's Subjective Logic, define the subjective opinion $\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$. Explain the **discounting operator** $\otimes$ used in trust transitivity. Derive the formula for A's derived trust in C, $\omega_{A \otimes B}^{\,C}$, when A has trust opinion $\omega_A^B$ in B and B has trust opinion $\omega_B^C$ in C. **Verify** the opinion-closure constraint.
>
> **(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider two social-network agents A and B with the following opinions about a target $x$:
> - $\omega_A = (0.7, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5)$
> - $\omega_B = (0.5, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5)$
>
> Compute the **consensus** $\omega_{A \oplus B}$ using the $\oplus$ operator. Comment on whether the resulting uncertainty is higher or lower than either input — and explain why, intuitively.

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition of opinion (1.5 marks):** A subjective opinion about proposition $x$ is the tuple $\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$ with $b_x, d_x, u_x \in [0,1]$, $a_x \in [0,1]$, satisfying $b_x + d_x + u_x = 1$. The four components represent belief, disbelief, uncertainty, and the base rate (a priori probability).

**Discounting operator description (1 mark):** The discounting operator $\otimes$ is used for **transitive trust** along a chain A → B → C. It models how A's trust in B *moderates* A's acceptance of B's recommendations about C.

**Derivation (4 marks):** As A's confidence in B is $b_A^B$, only this fraction of B's opinion is "transmitted" forward. A's derived belief in C is the product:

$$b_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot b_B^C$$

Similarly, A adopts B's disbelief in C to extent $b_A^B$:

$$d_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot d_B^C$$

A's uncertainty about C is the sum of (i) A's scaled ignorance about B's reliability and (ii) B's own uncertainty as filtered by A's trust:

$$u_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot u_B^C + u_A^B$$

The base rate is preserved:

$$a_{A||C} = a_B^C$$

**Closure Verification (0.5 marks):** Summing:

$$b_{A||C} + d_{A||C} + u_{A||C} = b_A^B(b_B^C + d_B^C + u_B^C) + u_A^B = b_A^B + u_A^B = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

(using $d_A^B = 0$ in the trust-chain interpretation, i.e., A's distrust of B is *not* a source of belief about C — only A's trust in B matters for forward propagation).

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Common denominator:**

$$u_A + u_B - u_A u_B = 0.2 + 0.3 - (0.2)(0.3) = 0.5 - 0.06 = 0.44$$

**Belief:**

$$b_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.7)(0.3) + (0.5)(0.2)}{0.44} = \frac{0.21 + 0.10}{0.44} = \frac{0.31}{0.44} \approx 0.7045$$

**Disbelief:**

$$d_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.1)(0.3) + (0.2)(0.2)}{0.44} = \frac{0.03 + 0.04}{0.44} = \frac{0.07}{0.44} \approx 0.1591$$

**Uncertainty:**

$$u_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.2)(0.3)}{0.44} = \frac{0.06}{0.44} \approx 0.1364$$

**Base rate:**

$$a_{A \oplus B} = \frac{(0.5)(0.3) + (0.5)(0.2)}{0.44} = \frac{0.15 + 0.10}{0.44} = \frac{0.25}{0.44} \approx 0.5682$$

**Verification:**

$$0.7045 + 0.1591 + 0.1364 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final fused opinion:**

$$\omega_{A \oplus B} = (0.7045,\ 0.1591,\ 0.1364,\ 0.5682)$$

**Intuitive comment (1 mark):** The uncertainty **decreased** from $u_A = 0.2$ and $u_B = 0.3$ to $u_{A \oplus B} \approx 0.1364$. This is because $\oplus$ *pools evidence* from two independent observers — having two witnesses is more informative than one. In the social-network context, this justifies the *reputation systems* that aggregate multiple peer reviews.

---

> ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
>
> **(a)** *(7 marks)* What is the **consensus operator** $\oplus$ in Subjective Logic? Derive the expression for the fused belief $b_{A \oplus B}$ when two independent agents A and B form opinions about the same target. State and justify the closure constraint.
>
> **(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider the trust chain **A → B → C** with the following opinions:
> - A's trust in B: $\omega_A^B = (0.8, 0.0, 0.2, 0.5)$
> - B's trust in C: $\omega_B^C = (0.6, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5)$
>
> Compute A's **derived transitive trust** in C, $\omega_{A \otimes B}^{\,C}$, using the $\otimes$ operator. Compute the **expected probability** of C being trustworthy. Explain what happens to the uncertainty in a 2-hop trust chain.

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Consensus operator definition (1.5 marks):** The consensus (or *fusion*) operator $\oplus$ combines two *independent* opinions $\omega_A = (b_A, d_A, u_A, a_A)$ and $\omega_B = (b_B, d_B, u_B, a_B)$ about the same proposition into a single fused opinion $\omega_{A \oplus B}$. It is used when multiple independent witnesses have evidence about the same target.

**Derivation of fused belief (3.5 marks):** Both opinions correspond to Beta-distribution posteriors with effective positive, negative, and prior counts $(r_A, s_A, \alpha_A)$ and $(r_B, s_B, \alpha_B)$. Independence means the joint posterior is Beta with summed counts: $(r_A + r_B,\ s_A + s_B,\ \alpha_A + \alpha_B)$. Translating back to opinion space:

$$b_{A \oplus B} = \frac{r_A + r_B}{r_A + r_B + s_A + s_B + \alpha_A + \alpha_B}$$

Substituting $r_X = b_X \cdot (r_X + s_X + \alpha_X)$ and factoring through, the cross-multiplied form is:

$$b_{A \oplus B} = \frac{b_A u_B + b_B u_A}{u_A + u_B - u_A u_B}$$

The cross-coupling (the $b_A u_B + b_B u_A$ numerator) reflects the Bayesian rule: belief from one source weighted by the *uncertainty* of the other.

**Closure constraint (2 marks):** The opinion components must sum to 1, i.e., $b_{A \oplus B} + d_{A \oplus B} + u_{A \oplus B} = 1$. This is a *fundamental axiom* of subjective opinions: belief, disbelief, and uncertainty together exhaust all epistemic states about a binary proposition. It guarantees that the operator outputs a *valid* opinion, which is required for any further downstream composition (e.g., using $\omega_{A \oplus B}$ as input to a discounting step).

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

Applying the $\otimes$ formulas:

**Derived belief in C:**

$$b_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot b_B^C = 0.8 \times 0.6 = 0.48$$

**Derived disbelief in C:**

$$d_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot d_B^C = 0.8 \times 0.1 = 0.08$$

**Derived uncertainty in C:**

$$u_{A||C} = b_A^B \cdot u_B^C + u_A^B = (0.8 \times 0.3) + 0.2 = 0.24 + 0.2 = 0.44$$

**Derived base rate in C:**

$$a_{A||C} = a_B^C = 0.5$$

**Verification (1 mark):** $0.48 + 0.08 + 0.44 = 1.00$ ✓

**Final derived opinion:**

$$\omega_{A \otimes B}^{\,C} = (0.48,\ 0.08,\ 0.44,\ 0.5)$$

**Expected probability of C being trustworthy (1 mark):**

$$P(\text{trust C}) = b_{A||C} + a_{A||C} \cdot u_{A||C} = 0.48 + 0.5 \times 0.44 = 0.48 + 0.22 = 0.70$$

**Uncertainty growth in 2-hop chains (1 mark):** Note that $u_{A||C} = 0.44$ is **larger** than *both* $u_A^B = 0.2$ and $u_B^C = 0.3$. In general, every additional hop in a trust chain *adds* uncertainty multiplicatively through the $b_A^B u_B^C$ term and additively through the $u_A^B$ term. This is why real-world trust systems **limit chain depth** to 2–3 hops — beyond that, uncertainty overwhelms the propagated belief and the opinion becomes vacuous.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the closure constraint** when writing the final answer: always verify $b + d + u = 1$ — failing this check costs **1 full mark** in most valuation schemes.
> 2. **Mixing up $\otimes$ and $\oplus$**: the *transitive* operator has a **multiplicative** structure on belief and an **additive** structure on uncertainty; the *consensus* operator has a **cross-coupling** structure. Confusing them yields **0 marks** for the derivation step.
> 3. **Ignoring the base rate** $a_x$ in the final answer: many students write only $(b, d, u)$ and skip $a$, losing a half-mark.
> 4. **Not justifying the "why"** behind operator design: KTU 2024 scheme marks *reasoning* (not just formulas) under CO mapping to Bloom's Level 4/5.
> 5. **Applying the consensus formula when $u_A = u_B = 0$**: this causes a **division by zero**. Always check the special-case boundary conditions first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Subjective Logic Trust Models**

- **Opinion Tuple:** $\omega_x = (b_x, d_x, u_x, a_x)$ with $b + d + u = 1$.
- **Belief $b$:** Evidence *for*; **Disbelief $d$:** Evidence *against*; **Uncertainty $u$:** Lack of evidence; **Base rate $a$:** A priori expectation.
- **Projected Probability:** $P(x) = b + a u$ — the bridge back to classical probability.
- **Discounting $\otimes$** (transitive trust): belief multiplies, uncertainty adds the residual.
- **Consensus $\oplus$** (independent fusion): cross-coupled by uncertainty terms; **reduces** uncertainty.
- **Boundary cases:** Vacuous opinion ($u = 1$) is the *identity* for $\oplus$ and the *annihilator* for $\otimes$ (in the sense that $b_A^B = 0$ gives full uncertainty in the next link).
- **Trust chain length:** Each additional hop *grows* uncertainty — cap chain depth at 2–3 in practice.
- **Independence assumption:** Consensus ($\oplus$) requires *independent* opinions; if A and B share a common source of evidence, the operator over-counts.
- **Industry relevance:** EigenTrust, PeerTrust, blockchain identity, e-commerce reputation, insider-threat detection.
- **Key trick for the exam:** Whenever given numerical opinions in a question, **always check the closure** $b + d + u = 1$ on the inputs before applying any operator.
- **Memorisation priority (high → low):** Discounting formula > Consensus formula > Projection > Closure > Boundary conditions.
- **Examiner favourite:** "Derive the consensus operator formula" — practice this once and you have a guaranteed 7-marker on the paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trust Network Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT863 - SOCIAL NETWORK ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trust Network Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Trust Network** is a directed, weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ where each vertex $v \in V$ represents an autonomous agent (user, service, or entity) and each directed edge $e = (u, v) \in E$ encodes a quantified opinion of trust that agent $u$ holds about agent $v$. The weight function $w : E \rightarrow \mathcal{T}$ maps every edge to a value in a trust domain $\mathcal{T}$ (which may be discrete, continuous, or probabilistic).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Trust Network Analysis** is the computational study of how subjective beliefs about the reliability, integrity, and competence of agents propagate, compose, and evolve over a social or computer-mediated network, with the objective of supporting decision-making in the absence of direct experience.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 4)**
> Trust is treated as a *contextual, asymmetric, partial, and transitive (within limits)* relationship. Trust is **NOT** a property of an individual — it is a property of the **relationship** between a trustor and a trustee within a specific context (e.g., trust in movie reviews vs. trust in financial advice).

## 1.2 Key Properties of Trust (Exam-Critical)

| Property | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Asymmetry** | $w(u,v) \neq w(v,u)$ in general | Alice trusts Bob, but Bob does not necessarily trust Alice |
| **Context-Specificity** | Trust value depends on the domain | High trust for movies, low trust for medical advice |
| **Partiality** | Trust need not be total or zero; it admits intermediate values | A score of $0.7$ on a $[0,1]$ scale |
| **Subjectivity** | Trust is an opinion, not a measured quantity | Based on personal experiences and beliefs |
| **Transitivity (Limited)** | If A trusts B and B trusts C, A may trust C with some confidence | B is a *recommender* who attenuates the trust to C |
| **Path-Dependence** | The propagated trust value depends on the chosen path | A → B → C may yield a different value than A → D → C |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Village Gossip Chain**
> Imagine a village where nobody knows every newcomer personally. When a stranger (Trustee) wants to buy a goat, villagers (Trustors) do not transact directly. Instead, they ask a chain of trusted neighbours. Each villager *attenuates* the recommendation: even if a friend highly recommends the stranger, the villager's confidence is less than the friend's confidence. This attenuation along a chain is exactly how **trust propagation** works in computational trust networks. The shorter and more reputable the chain, the higher the propagated trust.

**Concrete Example:** In a movie-rating site, when User A has not seen a film rated by User C, A may look at User B (whom A trusts) and ask: "Do you trust C's taste?" If B trusts C highly in the "movie reviews" context, A can adopt an opinion of C, scaled down by A's trust in B.

## 1.4 The Two Faces of Trust

- **Direct Trust:** $D_{u,v}$ — the trust that $u$ places in $v$ based on direct past interactions.
- **Indirect (Propagated / Recommender) Trust:** $I_{u,v}$ — the trust inferred through one or more intermediaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Total trust from $u$ to $v$ is often modeled as:
>
> $$T_{u,v} = (1-\alpha)\, D_{u,v} \;+\; \alpha\, I_{u,v}, \qquad \alpha \in [0,1]$$
>
> where $\alpha$ is the **propagation weight** controlling how much the agent relies on social recommendations versus direct experience.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Trust Representation Models

A trust relationship must first be encoded numerically. The KTU syllabus recognises the following three families:

### 2.1.1 Binary Trust
The simplest model: $w \in \{0, 1\}$. A user is either *trusted* or *not trusted*. This loses information and is rarely used in modern SNA.

### 2.1.2 Discrete Trust (e.g., Advogato)
A finite set of trust levels, for example $\mathcal{T} = \{ \text{Newcomer, Apprentice, Journeyer, Master} \}$. Each level can be mapped to a numeric rank to enable arithmetic.

### 2.1.3 Continuous / Probabilistic Trust
$w \in [0, 1]$ (or $[-1, +1]$ when distrust is allowed). Probabilistic models use **Subjective Logic** with opinion quadruples $\omega = (b, d, u, a)$ where $b$ is belief, $d$ is disbelief, $u$ is uncertainty, and $a$ is the base rate.

## 2.2 Trust Propagation — The Two Core Operators

Propagation involves **concatenation** (along a path) and **aggregation** (across multiple paths).

### 2.2.1 Concatenation Operator (Path Composition)
